name: HMS Unbeaten (N 93)
events: The history of HMS Unbeaten as compiled on this page is extracted from the patrol reports and logbooks of this submarine. Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
As no logs are available for HMS Unbeaten from February 1942 until her loss, details for this period will be missing.
This page dealing with the wartime history of HMS Unbeaten was compiled in March 2013.
This page was last updated in December 2025.
There is a nice website about HMS Unbeaten, you can find it here.
18 Nov 1940 Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted compass adjustment trials at Barrow.
Around 1230 A/18, she departed her builders yard at Barrow for Holy Loch. She was escorted by HMS Troubadour (Cdr.(ret) D.H. Milward, DSC, RNR). (1)
19 Nov 1940 Around 1800 A/19, Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) arrived at Holy Loch from Barrow. En-route various trails had been carried out. (1)

20 Nov 1940 Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted her acceptance trials in Gare Loch following which she returned to Holy Loch and was officially accepted from the shipyard and placed in full commission.

[Other sources give the commissioning day as 10 November 1940 but usually ships were placed in full commission on successful completion of their acceptance trials so in that case 20 November seem to be correct] (1)
22 Nov 1940 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted independent exercises in Lower Loch Long. (1)
23 Nov 1940 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted independent exercises in Lower Loch Long. (1)
24 Nov 1940 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted independent exercises in Lower Loch Long. (1)
25 Nov 1940 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted independent exercises in the Clyde area. (1)
27 Nov 1940 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) proceeded from Holy Loch to Arrochar where torpedo discharge trials were to be carried out on the torpedo firing range. (1)
28 Nov 1940 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (1)
29 Nov 1940 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (1)
30 Nov 1940 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. Upon completion of these trials, HMS Unbeaten, proceeded to Holy Loch. (1)
1 Dec 1940 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area in which the Dutch torpedo boat HrMs Z 8 (Lt.Cdr. P.A. de Boer, RNN) served as target. (2)

3 Dec 1940 HMS Tuna (Lt.Cdr. M.K. Cavenagh-Mainwaring, DSO, RN) and HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area in which HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A. E. Johnston, RN) and HrMs Z 8 (Lt.Cdr. P.A. de Boer, RNN) served as targets. (2)

4 Dec 1940 HMS Oberon (Lt.Cdr. E.F. Pizey, DSO, RN) and HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area with each other and also with HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN). (3)
5 Dec 1940 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted noise trials in Loch Long. (2)
6 Dec 1940 During a gale, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) broke adrift at Holy Loch. She collided with a merchant vessel that was anchored nearby and then grounded. (2)

8 Dec 1940 At Holy Loch, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) was refloated afer her grounding two days ago. She had sustained no damage. A diver had inspected the hull and keel and a docking was not required. (2)
12 Dec 1940 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) is wiped (degaussed) at Holy Loch. (2)
13 Dec 1940 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted a deep dive trial in lower Loch Long. A slight leak was found at no.79 frame. (2)
15 Dec 1940 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted minelaying trials in Loch Striven. The mines were laid from the torpedo tubes. (2)

16 Dec 1940 Around 1455 A/16, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Holy Loch for Barrow. She returned to her builders for repairs to her Kingstons and the fitting of new propellers. Before departure compass adjustment trials were carried out.
She was escorted by HMS White Bear (Cdr.(Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN). (2)
17 Dec 1940 Around 1350 A/17, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) arrived at Barrow. (2)
19 Dec 1940 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) is docked at her builder yard at Barrow. (2)
26 Dec 1940 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) is undocked. (2)
27 Dec 1940 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) is wiped (degaussed) at Barrow. (2)
29 Dec 1940 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted compass adjustment trials at Barrow. (2)
30 Dec 1940 Around 1130 A/30, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Barrow for Dartmouth. She was escorted by HMS Philante (Capt.(Retd.) H.S. Bowlby, RN). (2)
1 Jan 1941 Around 1615 A/1, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) arrived at Dartmouth. HMS Unbeaten was to be used for (A/S) training. (4)

2 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Dartmouth with HMS Mistral (Cdr. C.H. Brooks, RAN) and later with HrMs Notre Dame de France (Lt. J.H. van de Weijer, RNN(R)) and HrMs Jean Frederic (Lt. F.A. Staverman, RNN(R)). (4)

3 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Dartmouth with HMS Ruby (T/Lt. R.P. Chapman, RNR) and HMS Jasper (T/Lt. G.T.S. Clampitt, RNR). The exercises were soon abandoned due to bad weather.
At 1805 A/3, HMS Unbeaten left Dartmouth for Portsmouth. She was not escorted. (4)

4 Jan 1941 Around 1215 A/4, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) arrived at Portsmouth / Gosport, Haslar Creek (HMS Dolphin) where she was immediately docked in AFD 2 for yet another change of her propellers. (4)
6 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) is undocked. (4)
7 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Portsmouth with HMS Vanoc (Lt.Cdr. J.G.W. Deneys, RN) and HMS Wilna (Cdr.(Retd.) A.D.D. Smyth, DSO, RN). (4)
8 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted independent exercises off Portsmouth. These were followed by A/S exercises with HMS Wilna (Cdr.(Retd.) A.D.D. Smyth, DSO, RN). (4)
9 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Portsmouth with HMS Radiant (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) A.J. Brewster, RN) and HMS Kingston Amber (A/Skr.Lt. J. Flett, RNR). (4)
10 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted exercises off Portsmouth.
Later the same day was docked again in AFD 2 at Gosport / Haslar Creek for yet another change of her propellers. During the night an air raid took place but Unbeaten was not damaged. (4)
11 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) is undocked. (4)

13 Jan 1941 Around 1440 A/13, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Gosport / Haslar Creek (Portsmouth) for Dartmouth. She was escorted until around 1740 A/13 by HMS Kingston Agate (T/Lt. J.B. Elmsley, RCNVR). (4)
14 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Dartmouth with HMS Jupiter (Lt.Cdr. N.V.J.T. Thew, RN), HMS Kashmir (Cdr. H.A. King, RN) and A/S trawlers. (4)
15 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Dartmouth with HMS Jupiter (Lt.Cdr. N.V.J.T. Thew, RN), HMS Kashmir (Cdr. H.A. King, RN) and A/S trawlers.
Following these exercises the destroyers proceeded to Plymouth where they arrived at 1452 A/15. (4)
16 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Dartmouth with HNoMS Mansfield (Cdr. F. Ulstrup, RNorN) and HMS Cornelian (T/Lt. H.F. Hayes, RNVR). (4)

17 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Dartmouth with HrMs Notre Dame de France (Lt. J.H. van de Weijer, RNN(R)), HrMs Jean Frederic (Lt. F.A. Staverman, RNN(R)) and three ML's.
On completion of these exercises HMS Unbeaten set course for Portsmouth. (4)
18 Jan 1941 Around 1200 A/18, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) arrived at Portsmouth / Gosport, Haslar Creek.
She was escorted in. The escort had joined around 0900 A/18. (4)
20 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Portsmouth with HMS Wilna (Cdr.(Retd.) A.D.D. Smyth, DSO, RN). (4)
21 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Portsmouth with HMS Wilna (Cdr.(Retd.) A.D.D. Smyth, DSO, RN). (4)

22 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Portsmouth with HMS Berkeley (Lt.Cdr. H.G. Walters, RN) and HMS Cleveland (Lt.Cdr. W.S. Clouston, RN). These A/S exercises were followed by independent exercises. (4)
23 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) is docked once more in AFD 2 at Gosport / Haslar Creek for yet another change of her propellers. She was undocked later the same day. (4)
24 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted independent exercises off Portsmouth. (4)

28 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted independent exercises off Portsmouth. These independent exercises were followed by A/S exercises with HMS Cambridgeshire (T/Lt. G.S. Woolley, RNVR). (4)
29 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Portsmouth with HMS Arctic Pioneer (T/Lt.Cdr. A. Harrison, RNR). (4)
30 Jan 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Portsmouth with HMS Sapphire (T/Lt. F.R.H. Swann, RNVR) and HMS Kingston Amber (A/Skr.Lt. J. Flett, RNR).
[No logs are available of HMS Unbeaten for the months of February, March and April 1941. Details for these months will be missing.] (4)
10 Feb 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Portsmouth for Dartmouth. (5)

11 Feb 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) arrived at Dartmouth. A/S exercises were then carried out with HNoMS Mansfield (Cdr. F. Ulstrup, RNorN), HMS Clare (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) C. Gwinner, RN) and HMS Chesterfield (Lt.Cdr. E. Gleave, RNR). (6)

12 Feb 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Dartmouth with HNoMS Mansfield (Cdr. F. Ulstrup, RNorN), HMS Clare (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) C. Gwinner, RN) and HMS Chesterfield (Lt.Cdr. E. Gleave, RNR). (7)
13 Feb 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Dartmouth with HMS Burwell (Lt.Cdr. S.R.J. Woods, RNR) and HMS Reading (Cdr. R. Heathcote, RN). (7)
15 Feb 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Dartmouth with four ML's upon completion of which HMS Unbeaten set course for Portsmouth. (5)
16 Feb 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) arrived at Portsmouth. (5)
3 Mar 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Portsmouth for Dartmouth. (5)

4 Mar 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) arrived at Darmouth and conducted A/S exercises with HMS Salisbury (Lt.Cdr. H.M.R. Crichton, RN) and HMS Castleton (Cdr. (Retd.) F.H.E. Skyrme, RN). (8)
5 Mar 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Dartmouth with HMS Salisbury (Lt.Cdr. H.M.R. Crichton, RN) and HMS Castleton (Cdr. (Retd.) F.H.E. Skyrme, RN). (9)
6 Mar 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Dartmouth with HMS Richmond (Lt.Cdr. A.F.L. Evans, RN) and HMCS Niagara (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) E.L. Armstrong, RCN). (9)
9 Mar 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Dartmouth for Portsmouth. No more A/S exercises had been carried out since the 6th due to the unsuitable weather conditions. (8)
10 Mar 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) arrived at Portsmouth. (5)
19 Mar 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Portsmouth for Dartmouth. (5)
20 Mar 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) arrived at Dartmouth for more A/S exercises. (5)

21 Mar 1941 No A/S exercises for HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) though. She departed Dartmouth at 0400 A/21 for her 1st war patrol off Brest, France in the vicinity of position 47°40'N, 06°10'W. The German battlecruisers Scharnhorst and Gneisenau which had been operating in the Atlantic were expected to proceed to Brest.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (10)

21 Mar 1941 In a signal timed 1138 A/21, the Flag Officer Submarines sent the following order to HMS Union (Lt. R.M. Galloway, RN) and HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN); If weather is thick take every opportunity of pressing on the surface. (11)

21 Mar 1941 In a signal timed 1406 A/21, the Flag Officer Submarines informed HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. H.F. Bone, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Union (Lt. R.M. Galloway, RN) and HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) of the following; U-Boat sighted at 1156 Z/21 in position 47°00'N, 10°00'W. Enemy was steering westwards.
This may have referred to the German submarine U-97. (11)

24 Mar 1941 Around 1800 A/24, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) received the Flag Officer Submarines signal timed 1709 A/24 which gave a new patrol position. Course was altered to comply with these orders.
[We currently do not have the text of this signal, further research is required.] (10)
26 Mar 1941 At 1602 A/26, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) set course for a new patrol position in accordance with the Flag Officer Submarines signal timed 1201 A/26.
27 Mar 1941 At 1658 A/27, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) set course for a new patrol position in accordance with the Flag Officer Submarines signal timed 1609 A/26.
30 Mar 1941 At 2130 A/30, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) set course to leave patrol in accordance with the Flag Officer Submarines signals timed 0031 A/28 and 1401 A/29.
4 Apr 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) ended her 1st war patrol at Portsmouth. She had sighted only fishing vessels during her patrol.
She was escorted in by HMS Cape Palliser (T/Lt. D.C. Hayes, RNVR) which had joined around 0600 A/4. (10)

12 Apr 1941 Around 1730 A/12, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Portsmouth for Gibraltar. She was to proceed to Malta to join the U-class submarines of the 1st Submarine Flotilla based there.
She was escorted out, [identity of the escort not known to us] until 2130 A/12.

HMS Unbeaten was to proceed through the following positions; 180° - St. Catherines - 2 miles (A) 212° - St. Catherines - 12 miles (B) 180° - Wolf Rock - 12 miles (C) 45°40'N, 09°16'W (D) 40°00'N, 11°00'W (E) 36°40'N, 09°30'W (at 0600 A/20) 350° - Cape Spartel - 10 miles (at 0600 A/21)
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.]

From position 180° - St. Catherines - 2 miles she was to proceed dived by day as far as position (C) 45°40'N, 09°16'W. From that position she could proceed on the surface at the Commanding Officers discretion. An escort would be met at 0600 A/21 in position 350° - Cape Spartel - 10 miles for onward passage to Gibraltar. (10)

14 Apr 1941 At 0230 A/14, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted a submarine in position 49°54'N, 04°45'W. This turned out to be HMS H 31 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson DSC, RN) which was about 20 miles out of position, they exchanged recognition signals. (10)

16 Apr 1941 In a signal timed 1010 A/16, F.O.C.N.A. (Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic) ordered HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN), HMS Union (Lt. R.M. Galloway, RN) and HMS Urge (Lt. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) to report their position, course and speed of advance on crossing Latitude 41°N., They were also informed that two British trawlers were patrolling off Lisbon.

In a signal timed 1547 A/16, they were informed by F.O.S. (Flag Officer Submarines) that a German U-boat was in position 37°N, 14°W (west of Cape St. Vincent) at 2332 A/15. This most likely referred to U-38 but she was actually a bit further to the west moving westwards. (12)

17 Apr 1941 At 0800 A/17, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted a Breton Grand Banker barquentine in position 45°15'N, 09°22'W, steering 330°. The weather was unsuitable for a gun attack and the vessel was not deemed worth of a torpedo so the attack was abandoned. (10)

20 Apr 1941 Around 1700 A/20, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN), which had been ahead of the original schedule, arrived at Gibraltar. [Nothing can be found of a possible escort to Gibraltar from the vicinity of Cape Spartel.] (10)
23 Apr 1941 Around 1800 A/23, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Gibraltar for Malta.
[On the 26th she was ordered to patrol off Marittimo Island making this passage her 2nd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean).]
[No log is available for this period. The map displayed is made with position found in the patrol report.]
For some positions of HMS Unbeaten during this patrol see the map below.
26 Apr 1941 In a signal timed 1254 A26, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) was ordered to patrol in area 'E' (37°50'N, 11°30'E - 000° - 30 - 30) to intercept an important enemy convoy.

This was to intercept a convoy made up of the German transports Marburg (7564 GRT, built 1928), Kybfels (7764 GRT, built 1937), Reichenfels (7744 GRT, built 1936) and the Italian transports Birmania (5305 GRT, built 1930) and Rialto (6099 GRT, built 1927) escorted by the Italian destroyers Fulmine, Euro and the Italian torpedo boats Castore, Canopo and Procione [note: the Italian torpedo boat Orione did not sail with this convoy, it is an error in the Supermarina Diaries which also include this vessel as escort. She was supposed to have sailed with the convoy but did not make it, most likely due to defects], which sailed from Naples at 0000 B/25-0235 B/25 and stopped at Palermo (2057 B/25-0455 B/28) before sailing again this time with the addition of the Italian liner / troop transport Marco Polo (12272 GRT, built 1912) and the Italian torpedo boat Orsa.

The convoy then split in two: 1) Birmania, Rialto, Fulmine and Euro entered Messina (1850 B/28-0500 B/30. 2) Marco Polo, Marburg, Kybfels, Reichenfels, Castore, Canopo, Orsa and Procione entered Augusta 1950 B/28-0115 B/30). They reunited at 0800 B/30 before proceeding to Tripoli. At 1850 B/1 May, they sighted the two cruiser divisions which were protecting their left flank. They arrived at their destination at 2120 B/1-2300 B/1 (May).

The convoy was covered by: 1) The heavy cruisers Trento and Bolzano (Third Cruiser Division) screened by the destroyers Vincenzo Gioberti, Ascari and Carabiniere which sailed from Messina at 1330 B/30. 2) The light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia and Emanuele Filiberto Duca d'Aosta (Seventh Cruiser Division) screened by the destroyers Nicolò Zeno, Alvise da Mosto, Giovanni da Verazzano and Antonio Pigafetta which sailed from Taranto at 0400 B/30-0535 B/30 and arrived at Augusta at 0600 B/2-0755 B/2. (12)
29 Apr 1941 At 0200 A/29, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) arrived in her assigned patrol area. (10)

30 Apr 1941 In a signal timed 1133 B/30, the Senior Officers Submarines Malta, ordered HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) and HMS Usk (Lt. G.P. Darling, RN) to leave patrol P.M./30 so as to arrive at Malta around 0600 B/3.
At 2115 B/30, HMS Unbeaten left her patrol area to comply with these orders. (12)
3 May 1941 Around 0600 A/3, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) ended her 2nd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (10)

11 May 1941 Around 2115 A/11, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Malta for her 3rd war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in area 'V' to the east off Tripoli, Libya (south of 33°20'N and between 13°40'E and 15°00'E).
En route to the patrol area she was to proceed through position 035°31'N, 14°13'E and 35°00'N, 14°30'E, thence 180° to area 'V'.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS Unbeaten during this patrol see the map below.
14 May 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) attacked a large schooner in a convoy with three torpedoes West of Khoms, Libya. The schooner was claimed sunk but this was not the case.

This convoy must almost certainly have been the one made up of the schooners V 84 / Alas (244 GRT, built 1935), V 137 / Trio F. (244 GRT, built 1921), Rita (?) and Neptunus (392 GRT, built 1919) escorted by the auxiliary gunboat Mario Bianco (258 tons, built 1911).

The five vessels sailed from Tripoli at 0700 B/14 for Benghazi. Alas and Rita arrived at the destination at 1500 B/24, Trio F. arrived at 1630 B/26, Neptunus ran aground on the way but was later freed. We are not sure when Mario Bianco arrived, probably either 24 or 26 May. The attack appears to have been unobserved.

1323 B/14 - Sighted masts of a convoy bearing 236°, range 12000 yards. They were close inshore proceeding eastwards. Closed at speed. The convoy was later seen to consist of one armed trawler escorting a 3-masted auxiliary schooner of about 1100 tons, a 3-masted auxiliary schooner of about 700 tons, a 2-masted auxiliary schooner of about 400 tons and one Dutch schoote of about 500 tons. They were proceeding east along the 5-fathom line about one mile off shore.
1453 B/14 - In approximate position 32°54'N, 13°54'E fired one salvo of three torpedoes from 4500 yards at the largest schooner. A faint explosion was heard about three minutes after firing.

1510 B/14 - Returned to periscope depth. Saw the armed trawler had turned back and closing at slow speed at a range of 4000 yards. The target however was not seen and is thought to have sunk. After 10 minutes the trawler turned back towards the convoy. (13)
15 May 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) damaged a schooner with gunfire off Khoms, Libya.

This was the schooner G 19 / Giorgio (196 GRT, built 1912) which had just arrived in Khoms and had first been sighted at 1700 hours, she was actually hit by a single shell which failed to explode. Some shells fell in the town but three of them were found unexploded.
2049 A/15 - Surfaced off Khoms harbour, 1000 yards from the 800 tons, three-masted schooner that was anchored there. She was a 3-masted schooner of about 800 tons.
2054 A/15 - Opened fire at a range of 700 yards. At least four hits were obtained and six rounds went over the target in the town beyond.
2058 A/15 - Ceased fire and cleared the area as to not overstay our welcome so close inshore. The target was thought to be settling by the stern and a small boat was seen to get clear. (13)

16 May 1941 At 1835 B/16, close inshore between Misurata and Khoms, while in position 32°27'N, 14°46'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted two large transports of about 9000 tons each, escorted by a Folgore-class destroyer. The enemy was proceeding westwards and the destroyer was zigzagging 40° either side of the mean course. She was stationed about 30° on the starboard bow of the leading transport. Lt. Woodward decided to attack from inside the screen.

However, while manoeuvring to do so, HMS Unbeaten struck bottom with 35 feet on the gauge. This forced her up to 20 feet and as the range to the destroyer was only 2000 yards it was believed she must have been sighted. by the time trim was recovered and another look could be made the destroyer was only 600 yards away and coming straight towards. The conclusion was drawn that she was coming in to attack and the attack was broken off. There was only eight feet of water underneath the keel. Evasive action was taken and water-tight doors were shut. The bottom was found at 60 feet. No depth charges however were dropped and it was later thought the destroyer had just come to the end of a leg in her zig and that she had not, after all, sighted the submarine. The opportunity to attack was now gone.

The convoy sighted was made up the German transports Tilly M. Russ (1600 GRT, built 1926) and Brook (1225 GRT, built 1927) and was on passage from Benghazi (departed 2000 B/14) for Tripoli (arrived 0635 B17-0646 B/17). The convoy was escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Castore. The transports were much smaller than estimated by Lt. Woodward.

At 1925 B/16, an Orsa-class torpedo boat was sighted. An attack had been started but this was broken off when it was seen to be an escort vessel. The enemy was proceeding eastwards at 30 knots about 3 miles from the coast.

This was most likely the torpedo boat Climene which sailed from Tripoli at 1404 B/16 to meet and escort the Italian transport Bosforo (3648 GRT, built 1929) arriving from Benghazi. [Bosforo had not been escorted on leaving Benghazi.]
At 1951 B/16, HE was heard on bearing 250° but nothing could be seen.

HMS Unbreaten retired to the north to clear the area and pass an enemy report (timed 1815 B/16) which was successfully passed at 0041 B/17. After passing the enemy report HMS Unbeaten cleared that area and proceeded westwards. (10)

18 May 1941 At 0950 B/18, close inshore between to the west of Khoms, while in approximate position 32°46'N, 14°05'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) picked up faint but fast HE on bearing 145° and moving eastward. Periscope visibility was only just a little over a mile and nothing could be seen. (10)

19 May 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) attacked the Italian transport Silvio Scaroni (1367 GRT, built 1921) off Tagiura, Libya in position 32°46'N, 14°06'E with a spread of three torpedoes. Two torpedoes however hit bottom and exploded exposing the stern of the submarine above the water. A hunt by the escorting destroyer followed in which Unbeaten was depth charged.

The escort was the Italian torpedo boat Pleiadi which sighted a torpedo which appeared to be aimed at her and combed the track while dropping depth charges. She remained hunting in the area for a long time and expended all her depth charges.
The small convoy had been on passage from Tripoli (departed 0550 B/19) to Benghazi. Silvio Scaroni carried 980 tons of fuel (gasoline) and ammunition.

At 0530 B/20, Pleiadi was relieved by the Italian torpedo boat Climene (probably because she had no more depth charges) which had sailed from Tripoli at 1640 B/19. Pleiadi returned to Tripoli at 1540 B/20.
Silvio Scaroni arrived at Benghazi at 1200 B/21. Climene did not enter Benghazi and returned immediately to Tripoli where she arrived at 1105 B/22.?
0435 B/19 - Sighted light bearing 025°. Closed at full speed to investigate.
0500 B/19 - Dived to the north-west of Khoms in position 32°56'N, 14°03'E as dawn was approaching. Continued to close the contact.

0540 B/19 - Identified the contact as an Italian hospital ship similar to the Galilea (Italian, 8040 GRT, built 1916). Visibility was poor, only about 1.5 miles and decreasing. Continued to close to endeavour to ascertain the ships name but the vessel was lost in the fog. Enemy course was 200° at only 2 knots.

This was most probably the hospital ship Sicilia (Italian, 9646 GRT, built 1924) which had sailed from Augusta at 2015 B/17. The torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari had sailed from Tripoli at 1932 B/18 to pilot her in and they arrived at Tripoli at 1105 B/19.
1219 B/19 - In position 32°46'N, 14°06'E sighted a single merchant vessel of 5000 to 6000 tons escorted by a destroyer proceeding eastwards. Closed to attack.

1241 B/19 - Fired three torpedoes from 3500 yards. The first torpedo however struck bottom and exploded 8 seconds after firing. The result was that the third torpedo was fired with Unbeaten out of control and possible even with her stern out of the water (and the bow way down). This torpedo then also hit bottom and exploded. A depth charge attack by the destroyer now followed. In all 27 depth charges were dropped, some of which were quite close causing minor damage. Unbeaten only surfaced about 12 hours later after the destroyer had finally left the scene. Unbeaten subsequently set course to return to Malta. (14)

19 May 1941 In a signal timed 1103 A/19, the Cdr. S.1 ordered HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) to leave patrol P.M./20 through position 33°20'N, 14°13'E, and 35°30'N, 14°13'E to arrive off Marsamxett about 0600 B/22. (15)
22 May 1941 At 0810 B/22, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean) at Malta.

[It is likely that HMS Unbeaten, which had sustained some damage from depth charges on the 19th and also had sustained some possible damage by the grounding on the 16th, was docked at Malta before proceeding on her next patrol but the ships log for May does not mention this and as there is no log for June available we can't check this for the time being.] (13)

11 Jun 1941 Around 1345 B/11, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Malta for her 4th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol south of the Straits of Messina and the east coast of Sicily.
On departure some independent exercises were carried out and also a few practice attacks were made on HMS Abingdon (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR).
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (13)
13 Jun 1941 At 1643 B/13, well to the south-south-east of Cape Spartivento, in position 37°39'N, 16°14'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) picked up fast HE on bearing 340°.
At 1647 B/13, HMS Unbeaten was at periscope depth and saw a Folgore-class destroyer right astern on a course of 040°. Speed of the enemy was about 17 knots. Range was only about 100 yards.

This was the Italian destroyer Giovanni da Verazzano. She had sailed from Taranto at 1330 B/12 to relieve the Italian torpedo boat Antares which was escorting the Italian liner / troop transport Neptunia (19328 GRT, built 1932). They had sailed from Bari at 0500 B/12 for Naples; the liner / troop transport had originally sailed from Venice on 11 June). Giovanni da Verazzano sighted the two vessels at 1840 B/12, east of Cape Santa Maria di Leuca. Twenty minutes later, Antares left them to go to Brindisi. At 0950 B/13, the escort was reinforced by the Italian destroyer Carabiniere, which had sailed from Messina at 0825 B/13. They crossed the Straits of Messina. At 1220 B/13, when about 6 miles north of Cape Rasolcomo, Giovanni da Verazzano turned back and returned to Taranto at 0200 B/14.

In the evening, Leading stoker Sturman complained of headaches and lost consciousness. He passed away at about 0100 B/14. He was buried at sea at 0413 B/14 in approximate position 37°27'N, 16°24'E. (13)

16 Jun 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) fired four torpedoes at a big Italian troop transport, thought to be either Oceania (Italian, 19507 GRT, built 1933) or Neptunia (Italian, 19475 GRT, built 1932) at the southern entrance to the Straits of Messina in position in position 37°51'N, 15°26'E. The torpedoes however miss the target.

The convoy attacked was made up of the German transports Spezia (1825 GRT, built 1924), Trapani (1855 GRT, built 1926) and Livorno (1829 GRT, built 1924) escorted by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Città di Genova (5413 GRT, built 1930) proceeding from Bari to Catania. An escorting aircraft, Cant Z.501 No.10 of 184^ Squadriglia, sighted three torpedo tracks and dropped two bombs, claiming to have hit the submarine but this was not the case.
Trapani, Livorno and Spezia sailed from Bari at 1630 B/14-1800 B/14. Città di Genova sailed at 0930 B/15 from Gallipoli to escort them. They arrived at Catania at 1300 B/16-1415 B/16.
The target of this attack must have been the Città di Genova which was mistaken for a very large liner.

0819 B/16 - Sighted the masts of a convoy. 10 minutes later it was established that the convoy was made up of three merchant vessels of about 2000 tons and a very large liner. Lt. Woodward closed at high speed to attack the liner.
0909 B/16 - Fired four torpedoes from about 7400 yards. Two hits were claimed.
0926 B/16 - Nine depth charges were dropped. Lt.Cdr. Woodward decided to stay deep and only returned to periscope depth at 1200 hours to find nothing in sight except patrolling aircraft. (13)

18 Jun 1941 In a signal timed 1145 B/18, the Cdr. S.1 ordered HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) to try to intercept and attack the Vichy-French destroyer Vauquelin that was expected to pass through the Staits of Messina that day en-route to Beirut. The Vichy-French warship was however not sighted. (13)
19 Jun 1941 In a signal timed 1149 B/19, the Cdr. S.1 ordered HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) to leave patrol P.M./21 by reverse of THE outward route to arrive off Marsamxett at 0630 B/22. (16)
22 Jun 1941 At 0640 B/22, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) ended her 4th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (13)

24 Jun 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) was docked in No.2 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. When the dock was just dry information was received about an important enemy troop convoy leaving Naples for Tripoli through the Straits of Messina. The dock was flooded up again and at 2240 B/24, HMS Unbeaten departed Malta for her 5th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the east of Sicily in the vicinity of position 36°45'N, 16°02'E, to intercept and attack the expected convoy.

This must have referred to the convoy made up of the troop transports Esperia (Italian, 11398 GRT, built 1920), Marco Polo (Italian, 12567 GRT, built 1912), Oceania (Italian, 19507 GRT, built 1933) and Neptunia (Italian, 19475 GRT, built 1932) escorted by the destroyers Aviere, Geniere, Vincenzo Gioberti and Antonio da Noli. This convoy was on passage from Naples (sailed at 0400 B/25) to Tripoli. The torpedo boat Calliope had joined from Messina to reinforce the escort. Due to the enemy air activity the convoy broke off their passage and diverted to Taranto. A distant cover force made up of the heavy cruisers Trieste, Gorizia and escorted by the destroyers Corazziere, Carabiniere and Ascari was also deployed.

25 Jun 1941 From 1625 B/25 onwards, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) heard a large number of explosions, culminating with three loud ones at 2138 B/25. They were all to the northward on a fairly steady bearing and it was believed the convoy would pass through Unbeaten's position.

At 2108 B/25, the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1921 B/25 was received which gave the following infomation; Four large merchant ships in square formation with two destroyers two miles ahead and two destroyers on either beam and one astern. Position 37°30'N, 15°35'E at 1835 B/25. Enemy course 160° at a speed of 10 knots. This signal confirmed the convoy's close proximity.
At 2158 B/25, HMS Unbeaten surfaced.

At 2200 B/25, in position 36°42'N, 16°02'E, what is thought to be a small destroyer was sighted on bearing 350° at a range of only 400 yards. Visibility was very poor, only about one mile and very hazy. HMS Unbeaten dived to avoid detection. No HE was picked up and it was believed the enemy may have stopped or was proceeding very slowly to listen. Set course to the south and later to the south-south-east.
At 2335 B/25, still no HE was picked up so HMS Unbeaten surfaced and proceeded to remain in the vicinity of the expected line of advance of the convoy. (17)

26 Jun 1941 At 0213 B/26, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) heard a fast engine which sounded like a motor torpedo boat close to port. Visibility was about 1.5 miles but it was very misty. HMS Unbeaten dived to avoid detection. No HE was picked up though.
At 0250 B/26, HMS Unbeaten surfaced to find nothing in sight. She then proceeded to the southward at slow speed in the vicinity of the expected track of the convoy.

At 0405 B/26, the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 0331 B/26 was received. This stated the following; Convoy attacked around 2140 B/25 in position 36°54'N, 16°00'E force unknown probably two merchant ships escorted by seven destroyers.
At 0538 B/26, in position 36°35'N, 16°02'E, a single trawler was sighted on bearing 360° at a range of 6 miles. Unbeaten altered course to close.
At 0600 B/26, the trawler was seen to be hunting. No other vessel was in the vicinity. Lt. Woodward decided to retire to the southward, as the convoy must have either passed or turned back by then.
At 0845 B/26, a distant explosion was heard followed by another one at 0945 B/26.
In a signal timed 1120 B/26, the Cdr. S.1 ordered HMS Unbeaten to return to Malta by the north-east swept channel around 0630 B/27. Course was set to comply with these orders. (17)
27 Jun 1941 Around 0700 B/27, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) ended her 5th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
Most likely Unbeaten was docked immediately but as no log is available for June 1941 we are not sure. (13)
1 Jul 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) was undocked at the Malta Dockyard.
[As no log is available for June 1941 it is at the moment unknown to us when she was docked but most likely this was 27 June immediately upon returning from patrol.] (13)

4 Jul 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) conducted trials and exercises off Malta. Two practice attacks were made but the logbook does not state which ship acted as the target but most likely this was HMS Abingdon (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR). (18)

8 Jul 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Malta for her 6th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Lampedusa in area 'L', 34°15'N, 12°20'E - 335° - 60 - 50 until P.M. on the 11th.
From A.M. on the 12th she was to patrol in area 'X'.
Before proceeding on patrol exercises were carried out together with HMS Abingdon (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR). Two practice attacks were made. (13)
8 Jul 1941 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Unbeaten during her 6th war patrol see the map below.

10 Jul 1941 In a signal timed 1957 B/10, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) received the following orders; Postpone movement to area X. A patrol line was to be established as follows: 3 - 35°40'N, 11°55'E - 090° - 12 - 000° - 24. HMS Unbeaten in position (A) 35°40'N 11°55'E. HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) in position (B). HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) in position (C). The latter two submarines would leave Malta P.M./11. Submarines are not to be attacked after 2000 B/12 until further orders. Information received that a southbound convoy of six medium sized merchant ships escorted by six destroyers will be in latitude of Lampion around 2100 B/12.

This must have referred to the convoy (Naples - Tripoli) made up of the transports Ernesto (Italian, 7399 GRT, built 1914), Nita (Italian, 6803 GRT, built 1913), Castelverde (Italian, 5270 GRT, built 1919), Nirvo (Italian, 5270 GRT, built 1919), Aquitania (Italian, 4971 GRT, built 1924) and the tanker Panuco (Italian, 7751 GRT, built 1941) that were being escorted by the Italian destroyers Fuciliere, Alpino, Lanzerotto Malocello and the Italian torpedo boats Procione, Pegaso and Orsa.

They sailed from Naples at 1930 B/10-2200 B/10 for Tripoli, except Fuciliere and Alpino which sailed from Palermo at 1623 B/11-1630 B/11 and joined the convoy at 2020 B/11, in position 38°28'N, 12°22'E. At 2120 B/12, Panuco had an accidental fire, which was later controlled. Lanzerotto Malocello ordered Orsa to go to her assistance. At 1102 B/13, the torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari sailed from Tripoli and, at 1720 B/13 reinforced the escort. As they were close to Tripoli, a heavy fog which limited visibility to 500 metres which delayed their arrival. All ships from the convoy arrived in harbour at 0600 B/14-0810 B/14.
The sailing of the convoy was known from an ULTRA intercept, see file DEFE3/832, pp. 883-884. The destroyer Alpino was not correctly identified but listed as Albona (???). (text garbled). (19)

11 Jul 1941 In a signal timed 1804 B/11, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) of the following; Four destroyers, five merchant ships and one tanker in position 38°55'N, 12°40'E, course 200°, speed 9 knots at 1530 B/11. (19)

12 Jul 1941 In a signal timed 0915 B/12, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta gave the following orders to HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) and HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN). Cancel patrol line ordered. HMS Unbeaten patrol in position 35°40'N, 11°55'E and HMS P 33 patrol in position 35°50'N, 12°08'E until in contact with the enemy. Neither submarine is to cross 35°45'N. Final enemy report will be passed on the next Rugby operational routine. If speed allows HMS Unbeaten should also shadow and attack convoy during the night and then operate in area 'X'. (19)

12 Jul 1941 At 1513 B/12, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) heard the first of nine single explosions. The last was heard at 1650 B/12. Although it was very difficult to judge the direction, it appeared as if the convoy was closing.

At 1841 B/12, HMS Unbeaten was working on the last enemy report from 0650 B/12 and judging roughly by the explosions, it was believed that the convoy should be near. Visibility was down to 4 miles, so Lt. Woodward decided to surface to see if it was better on the surface. Nothing was in sight on surfacing. Surface visibility was found to be 6 miles.
Having found nothing in sight, also on the surface, HMS Unbeaten dived again at 1847 B/12.

At 2100 B/12, Lt. Woodward decided on the basis of the available information to proceed at best speed towards Tripoli in the hope of intercepting the convoy there. So, although it was still light, HMS Unbeaten surfaced at 2109 B/12 and proceeded. (13)
14 Jul 1941 By the mid-afternoon of the 14th, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) patrolled to the north-north-west of Tripoli in the vicinity of position 33°23'N, 12°58'E.

In a signal timed 2000 B/12, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta had informed HMS Unbeaten that a 14 knot convoy of six merchant ships and six destroyers was expected to leave Tripoli (Libya) around 1600 B/14 for Kerkenah and Pantellaria. Convoy may depart from position A or D off Tripoli.
The position chosen by Lt. Woodward was within 2.5 miles of either probable course of the convoy between Tripoli en Kerkennah and also to ensure a possibility to attack about two hours before dark.
Nothing was however seen or heard of the expected convoy.

This signal must have referred to the convoy made up of the transports Rialto (Italian, 6099 GRT, built 1927), Andrea Gritti (Italian, 6338 GRT, built 1939), Sebastiano Venier (Italian, 6311 GRT, built 1940), Barbarigo (Italian, 5293 GRT, built 1930) and Ankara (German, 4768 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the destroyers Lanzerotto Malocello, Alpino, Fuciliere and the Italian torpedo boats Procione, Pegaso and Orsa.

The sailing of the convoy was know due to an ULTRA decrypt (see DEFE3/832, page 830A). Five (not six) merchant ships were correctly identified. The escorts to be provided by the six which had arrived with the Ernesto convoy. They sailed from Tripoli at 1430 B/14-1700 B/14 and arrived at Naples at 1410 B/16-1449 B/16 (except Alpino and Fuciliere which were detached to Palermo where they arrived at 0400 B/16-0408 B/16 and Barbarigo which was sunk on the 15th). (20)
15 Jul 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) sinks a schooner with gunfire at Marsa Zuag roads, Libya. Another schooner was damaged.
According to Italian sources these were only local fishing vessels and only one was hit and suffered some damage with three wounded.

2000 B/15 - Surfaced in position 32°50'N, 12°26'E and opened fire on two 2-masted schooners sighted earlier in the day along with some tunny vessels. Fire was opened from 700 yards. 43 rounds were fired. 20 hits were obtained. One of the schooners was seen heeling over and then settling by the stern.
2010 B/15 - Dived and cleared the area to the northward.
2149 B/15 - Surfaced and proceeded at speed to the northward.
2213 B/15 - Sighted a motor torpedo boat bearing 010° at a range of only 400 yards. The enemy was proceeding on course 300° at a reasonable speed. HMS Unbeaten dived.

2244 B/15 - Heard HE of another motor torpedo boat passing astern. Lt. Woodward decided to get well clear of this area and then proceed back to Malta by the route ordered in the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1105 B/14 [we have been unable to find the text of this signal]. (13)
19 Jul 1941 At 0700 B/19, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) ended her 6th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (13)

28 Jul 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Malta for her 7th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the east of Tunisia in area 'J' (between Pantelleria and Lampedusa) on the convoy routes between Italy and Tripoli and intercept a fast (17.5 knots) northbound convoy near position 36°05'N, 11°47'E.
For the daily positions of HMS Unbeaten during this patrol see the map below.

30 Jul 1941 At 0449 B/30, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) dived in position 36°05'N, 11°47'E and kept diving patrol in the vicinity. This position was chosen as being close northward of the expected position of an expected convoy at 0600 B/30.

The expected convoy (Tripoli - Naples) was the one which was made up of the Italian troop transports Neptunia (19475 GRT, built 1932), Oceania (19507 GRT, built 1933) and Marco Polo (12567 GRT, built 1912) that were escorted by the Italian destroyers Aviere, Geniere, Camicia Nera, Alfredo Oriani and Vincenzo Gioberti.
The convoy had sailed from Tripoli at 1835 B/29-2024 B/29 and arrived at Naples at 0430 B/31-0716 B/31.
At 0753 B/29, the first of twelve intermittently dropped depth charges was heard. The last of which was heard to explode at 1050 B/29. (13)

31 Jul 1941 At 0034 B/31, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) received the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 2354 B/30, which stated the following; 14 Knots escorted southbound convoy due in position 36°50'N, 11°54'E at 1530 B/31 probably proceeding via Kerkennah.

This referred to the convoy made up of the transports Andrea Gritti (Italian, 6338 GRT, built 1939), Rialto (Italian, 6099 GRT, built 1927), Sebastiano Venier (Italian, 6311 GRT, built 1940) and Ankara (German, 4768 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the Italian destroyer Lanzerotto Malocello and the Italian torpedo boats Procione and Pegaso.
They had sailed from Naples at 0310 B/29 for Tripoli. The escort was later reinforced by the torpedo boat Orione which had sailed from Trapani.

HMS Unbeaten set course to patrol in the vicinity of position 36°31'N, 11°40'E well within sight of Pantelleria. This position was chosen as being favourable for the preferable route of the convoy and also allowed for the possibility of the convoy passing to the east of Pantelleria.
At 1610 B/31, the first of twelve depth charges was heard. The last was heard to explode at 1734 B/31.
At 1616 B/31, in position 36°32'N, 11°49'E, the masts of one vessel were sighted on bearing 075° well down over the horizon. Unbeaten altered course to 145° to close.
At 1621 B/31, masts of the convoy were seen on bearing 080°. Range to the nearest vessel was 14500 yards. Unbeaten closed at speed.

At 1633 B/31, it was seen that the convoy had altered course away from 205° from 140°. Unbeaten altered course to 120° and continued to close. It was thought the submarine had been sighted by an aircraft at 1628 B/31 when a Caproni CA 311 was close overhead.

At 1655 B/31, it was seen that the convoy had altered course back to the original 205°. Unbeaten altered course towards but the range, now 16000 yards, was too great for an attack and the attempt was abandoned at 1709 B/31. (13)

1 Aug 1941 At 0445 B/1, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) dived and proceeded to the southward to comply with the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1120 B/30 which had contained the following orders; Remain south of 36°N from 0001 B/2 to 0001 B/3. Thee cruisers with destroyers will pass eastwards to Malta A.M. and westwards to Kolibia P.M. on 2/8.

This referred to 'Force X' which was made up of the light cruisers HMS Hermione (Capt. G.N. Oliver, RN) and HMS Arethusa (Capt. A.C. Chapman, RN), fast minelayer HMS Manxman (Capt. R.K. Dickson, RN) and escorted by the destroyers HMS Sikh (Cdr. G.H. Stokes, RN) and HMS Lightning (Cdr. R.G. Stewart, RN) which were to transport troops to Malta during operation 'Style'.

At 1314 B/1, HMS Unbeaten received the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1007 B/1 which ordered her to leave area 'J' and operate to the south of Lampedusa in area 'H' between Latitudes 34°50'N, and 35°40'N and Longitudes 11°40'E and 12°41'E. HMS Unbeaten continued to the southward to make landfall on Lampione Island on the way to area 'H'. (20)

4 Aug 1941 At 2145 B/4, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) surfaced in approximate position 35°06'N, 12°03'E and, after four blank days, Lt. Woodward decided to have a look at Lampedusa harbour. Course was set to the north-east. (13)

5 Aug 1941 At 0720 B/5, west of Lampedusa, in position 35°30'N, 12°16'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted two Spica-class torpedo boats apparently sweeping 1500 yards apart at a speed of 11 knots on a course of 315°. Unbeaten altered course due south to avoid.
The left wing torpedo boat passed 800 yards away. No A/S transmissions could be heard.

The torpedo boat Cigno and motor torpedo boat MAS 526 had sailed from Trapani at 1735 B/3 for an A/S sweep and arrived at Lampedusa at 1059 B/5. No other torpedo boats are known to have been in the area, except Antonio Mosto which was at Lampedusa with defects and sailed the next day for Palermo. Perhaps Woodward mistook MAS 526 for a Spica-class destroyer, perhaps he could not see it clearly and assumed it was a sister vessel to Cigno.

At 1657 B/5, in position 213° - Lampedusa harbour entrance - 2.2 miles. Several vessels were seen inside the harbour but unfortunately none of these vessels were lying favourable for a torpedo attack.

In view of this and the scarcity of torpedoes at Malta HMS Unbeaten altered course away and took no offensive action. She proceeded south to return to patrol in a position 17.5 miles east of Kerkennah No.4 buoy.

At 2217 B/5, in position 35°18'N, 12°40'E, distant HE was heard on bearing 060°. This persisted for 40 minutes but, although HMS Unbeaten altered course towards to cloase and the visibility was exceptionally good (the moon being full) nothing was sighted. (13)

6 Aug 1941 At 1706 B/6, south-west of Linosa, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) received the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1436 B/6 which stated the following; Six destroyers and six merchant Ships in position 210° - Pantellaria - 17 miles, course 170°, speed 15 knots at 1335 B/6.

This must have referred to the convoy made up of the Italian transports Aquitania (4971 GRT, built 1924), Nita (6803 GRT, built 1913), Castelverde (5270 GRT, built 1919), Ernesto (7399 GRT, built 1914), Nirvo (5270 GRT, built 1919), escorted by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Vincenzo Gioberti, Alfredo Oriani sailed from Naples at 1840 B/4-2025 B/4. The torpedo boat Calliope sailed from Palermo at 1455 B/5, followed by the destroyer Nicoloso da Recco at 1833 B/5, to reinforce escort. The Italian tanker Poza Rica (7799 GRT, built 1940) sailed from Trapani at 2000 B/5 to join. At 1130 B/6, Aviere sighted a submarine 4 miles south of Pantelleria. It was attacked with depth charges. At 2215 B/6, Nita was hit by a torpedo from a Swordfish of 830 Squadron and sank at 0133 B/7 (casualties: one missing) in position 35°15'N, 12°17'E. The remaining ships arrived at Tripoli at 1641 B/7-1902 B/7.
It was expected that this convoy would pass HMS Unbeaten's position around 1730 B/6.

At 2210 B/6, AA gunfire was seen on bearing 010°. As this indicated that the convoy was being attacked in a position further to the eastward than was expected, HMS Unbeaten proceeded to the south-east to intercept.
Later attacks on the convoy indicated a probable turn about, as the AA fire appeared to be further away. Unbeaten set course to the northward at speed to try to close.
At 0145 A/7, a burning wreck was seen bearing 015°. Unbeaten closed to investigate. [This must have been the wreck of the Nita that had been sunk by Allied aircraft.]

At 0310 A/7, the burning wreck and oil had been closed in position 35°16'N, 12°12'E. Nothing else was sighted. There was a strong smell of paraffin in the area. Lt. Woodward decided that if other vessels of the convoy would have been damaged they would probably proceed to the nearest port which was Lampedusa so Unbeaten proceeded eastwards towards the Lampedusa - Tripoli route and then went northward along it to Lampedusa.

Later, when in position 210° - Lampedusa harbour entrance - 2.8 miles it was seen that only a three-masted schooner appeared to be in the harbour. Unbeaten proceeded eastwards to leave the area and return to Malta in accordance with the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1317 B/5 which had contained the following orders; Leave patrol P.M./7th through position 35°40'N, 14°06'N, to arrive off Marsamxett at 0700 B/9. (21)
9 Aug 1941 Around 0700 B/9, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) ended her 7th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (13)

18 Aug 1941 At 1555 B/18, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Malta for her 8th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the north of Pantelleria in position 37°09'N, 11°58'E to intercept an important fast southbound enemy troop convoy.

19 Aug 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) fires three torpedoes (four were intended) against a convoy of Italian troop transports about 15 nautical miles north of Pantelleria. All torpedoes fired missed their target.

The convoy attacked was made up of the Italian troop transports (liners) Neptunia (19475 GRT, built 1932), Oceania (19507 GRT, built 1933), Marco Polo (12567 GRT, built 1912) and Esperia (11398 GRT, built 1920). They were initially escorted by the Italian destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Nicoloso da Recco, Vincenzo Gioberti and Alfredo Oriani. Later the destroyers Grecale, Maestrale and Scirocco joined from Palermo and the torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza joined from Trapani. An escorting CANT Z.501 from 196^Squadriglia reported the torpedo tracks (which crossed far ahead of the convoy) and dropped two bombs on the submarine.
1818 B/19 - In position 37°02'N, 12°00'E sighted the funnels of large merchant vessel bearing 325°. Range was about 9500 yards.
1822 B/19 - Sighted four liners in formation with three escorting destroyers on their port wing. Range was now 8000 yards.

1831 B/19 - Fired a salvo of three torpedoes. Four torpedoes were intended but the third torpedo of the salvo did not leave the torpedo tube. A loud explosion was heard 5 minutes and 25 seconds after firing. This gave a running range of 6500 yards exactly the range that was estimated on firing. The results could not be observed as Unbeaten had been taken deep on firing due to the heavy air escort overhead.
1940 B/19 - Picked up HE of a destroyer which was leaving the area to the south-westward.
1950 B/19 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight. (13)
20 Aug 1941 At 0430 B/20, in position 36°33'N, 13°10'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) picked up HE bearing 340°. Course was altered to close.

At 0453 B/20, a submarine was sighted bearing 030° at a range of 800 to 1000 yards. This was believed to be HMS Urge (Lt. E.P. Tomkinson, RN). Also both HMS Unbeaten and HMS Urge were under instructions that no submarines were to be attacked at night. HMS Unbeaten dived.
HMS Urge did not report sighting another submarine. She dived at 0540 B/20 in position 36°33'N, 13°07'E so in all likelihood the submarine sighted by HMS Unbeaten was indeed HMS Urge. (13)
21 Aug 1941 Around 0715 B/21, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) ended her 8th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (13)

24 Aug 1941 Around 2020 B/24, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Malta for her 9th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in area 'N', south of the Straits of Messina / east coast of Sicily.

24 Aug 1941 At 0434 B/24, in position 37°03'N, 18°18'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted the lights of a Vichy-French convoy reported in the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1117 B/24. HMS Unbeaten closed to investigate.
At 0441 B/24, the convoy was recognised as indeed being the reported Vichy-French ships. HMS Unbeaten dived and took avoiding action.

The convoy sighted was evacuation convoy 'B' coming from Syria. This convoy was made up of the following ships Providence (11196 GRT, built 1920), Maréchal Lyautey (8256 GRT, built 1925) and Florida (9149 GRT, built 1926).

This convoy had departed Toulon on 13 August 1941, arrived at Beirut on 19 August, departed Beirut on 22 August and arrived at Marseille on 28 August. On board the Providence were 1822 passengers, on board the Maréchal Lyautey were 1444 passengers and on board the Florida were 1700 passengers. In total there were thus 4966 passengers on board the ships of the convoy of which 4368 were military personnel. (13)
27 Aug 1941 At 0620 B/27, south of Cape dell'Armi, in position 37°49'N, 15°41'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted a hospital ship proceeding on course 160°.
This was the Italian Arno (8024 GRT, built 1912) which sailed from Naples at 1600 B/26 and arrived at Tripoli at 0945 B/28. (13)

27 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 1115 B/27, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta, informed HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) that a Vichy-French convoy, made up of three 10 knot ships, passes area 'N' through Messina at 1200 B/2 and Straits of Bonifacio A.M./4 and are not to be molested. (22)
28 Aug 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) attacked an Italian submarine with four torpedoes in position 37°51'N, 15°46'E. No hits were obtained.
This was the Adua on passage from Pola to Messina but the attack was unobserved.

1630 B/28 - In position 37°51'N, 15°46'E sighted a Cosenz-class torpedo boat to the eastward apparently sweeping. Closed to investigate but the destroyer turned away to the eastward and proceeded down the coast.
This was probably the torpedo boat Giuseppe Missori which sailed from Messina at 0615 B/28 for an A/S sweep and arrived at Augusta at 0337 B/29.

1638 B/28 - Unfortunately attention had been concentrated on this torpedo boat and it was only now that and Italian Balilla-class submarine was sighted making westwards along the coast at a range of 2500 yards. The best opportunity for attack was already gone but non the less four torpedoes were fired from about 3000 yards. No hits were obtained and all torpedoes exploded on the shore. (13)
29 Aug 1941 At 1700 B/29, in position 37°32'N, 15°56'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted a hospital ship proceeding to the north-north-west on course 340°.
This was the Italian Arno (8024 GRT, built 1912) which sailed from Tripoli at 1400 B/28 and arrived at Naples at 0830 B/30.

At 1714 B/29, a large four-masted 2000 tons schooner was sighted. HMS Utmost closed to investigate. The schooner was laying stopped in position 37°28'N, 15°49'E and looked suspicious with two large deck houses amidships, another on the poop and very high bulwarks. She was flying the Italian flag.
This was the Italian A/S schooner V 51 / Alfa (373 GRT, built 1916). [HMS Unbeaten torpedoed and sank her the following day.]
At the same time, a number of intermittend explosions were heard and at 1740 B/29, a Cosenz-class torpedo boat was sighted to the northward apparently hunting.

This was probably the torpedo boat Giuseppe Missori which sailed from Augusta at 1205 B/29 for an A/S sweep and arrived at Augusta at 0215 B/30 [she then sailed from Augusta at 0628 B/30 and arrived at Messina at 1030 B/30].
It looked like an A/S search was going on in the area and that the schooner was a Q-ship. Unbeaten commenced to clear the area to the south-eastward. (13)

30 Aug 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian A/S schooner V 51 / Alfa (373 GRT, built 1916) about 25 nautical miles south of Capo dell'Armi, Italy in position 37°36'N, 15°32'E. Of a crew of 15, three were missing, the twelve survivors (including five wounded) were picked up by the hospital ship Epomeo and landed in Augusta.
0223 B/30 - In position 37°15'N, 16°05'E, sighted a red flare bearing 070° at a range of approximately 2 miles. Unbeaten dived. Five minutes later a single depth charge was heard to explode.
0253 B/30 - A second, more distant, depth charge was heard to explode.
Lt.Cdr. Woodward decided to return to the vicinity of the suspected Q-ship to see if she was still operating there.
1020 B/30 - Sighted the four-masted schooner seen earlier and thought to be a Q-ship. Closed to attack.
1045 B/30 - The four-masted schooner was relieved by a two-masted schooner and got underway too the northward at 8 knots.
1200 B/30 - She stopped again. Closed with caution to observe.

1745 B/30 - Range was now 700 yards. In position 37°36'N, 15°52'E, fired two torpedoes at the target. She was hit and blown clean out of the water. Seven survivors were seen clinging to the wreckage. Also one life boat was seen to be afloat. Proceeded to the east to search for the two-masted schooner but this ship was not seen again. (13)

31 Aug 1941 At 1002 B/31, south of the toe of Italy, in approximate position 37°50'N, 15°54'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat apparently hunting along the swept channel from east to west.

Though no Spica-class torpedo boat this must once again have been the torpedo boat Giuseppe Missori which sailed from Messina at 2130 B/30 for an A/S sweep in the area of Cape Spartivento and arrived at Messina at 1430 B/31. (13)

31 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 1113 B/31, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) of the following; Vichy-French convoy passes south through Messina at 1000 B/2. Leave patrol P.M./2 by reverse of outward route to arrive at Malta 0700 B/4. (22)

1 Sep 1941 In a signal timed 2241 B/1, the Capt. S.10's sent the following orders to HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) and HMS Otus (Lt. R.M. Favell, RN); Five merchant ships and five destroyers expected to pass through position 37°36'N, 16°04'E on constant course 145° at 1800 B/2 and vicinity of position 33°50'N, 18°00'E on approximate course 245° at about 1800 B/3. HMS Unbeaten endeavour to intercept in the first position mentioned and HMS Otus in the second position mentioned. Leaving them at sunset if convoy had not been sighted.

This signal referred to the Andrea Gritti convoy. An ULTRA intercept from 31 August (see DEFE3/832/ pp. 446-447) had revealed that it would proceed at 13.5 knots and arrive at Tripoli at 2045 B/3. It would consist of the Italian transports Andrea Gritti (6338 GRT, built 1939), Sebatiano Venier (6406 GRT, built 1943), Rialto (6099 GRT, built 1927), Francesco Barbaro (6343 GRT, built 1940) and Vettor Pisani (6339 GRT, built 1939) screened by five destroyers (not named in the ULTRA signal). The time of arrival was later corrected to 1345 B/4 (DEFE3/832/pp. 444-445). The convoy sailed from Naples at 2140 B/1-2355 B/1, it was screened by the destroyers Nicoloso da Recco, Freccia, Folgore, Strale and Dardo. At 0046 B/3, Andrea Gritti was torpedoed and sunk by a Swordfish of 830 Squadron (FAA) in 37°33'N, 16°26'E, the destroyers Dardo and Strale stood by to rescue the survivors. Strale transferred two slightly wounded survivors to Dardo before rejoining the convoy. Francesco Barbaro was also torpedoed and damaged, Dardo took her in tow. At 1000 B/3, the destroyers Ascari and Lanciere joined them and shortly after the submarine chaser Albatros. At 1158 B/3, the tug Porto Recanati (220 GRT, built 1937) joined and at 1320 B/3, the ocean-going tug Titano (788 GRT, built 1913) but as it would take too much time to transfer the tow, Dardo kept the towing and brought her to Messina at 1930 B/3. The remaining ships of the convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1815 B/4-1920 B/4.
In a signal timed 0835 B/2, the Capt. S.10 cancelled the orders given in his signal timed 2241 B/1. (23)
2 Sep 1941 At 1330 B/2, in position 37.33'N, 16.02'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted two Vichy-French convoys passing through the area.
One was northbound and had been reported in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1115 B/27. The second was southbound and had been reported in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1113 B/31.

The northbound convoy the evacuation convoy 'C', made up of the Vichy-French liners Marrakech (6288 GRT, built 1914), Sinaia (8567 GRT, built 1922) and Groix (9957 GRT, built 1922). This convoy had departed Toulon on 19 August and arrived at Beirut on 28 August. At Beirut Marrakech embarked 733 passengers, Sinaia embarked 1867 passengers and Groix embarked 1562 passengers. Total number of passengers was 4162 of which 3674 were military personnel. The convoy departed Beirut on 29 August and arrived at Marseille on 5 September.

The southbound convoy the evacuation convoy 'Z' made up of the Vichy-French liners Djenne (8790 GRT, built 1931) and Koutoubia (8790 GRT, built 1931). This convoy had departed Toulon on 28 August and arrived at Beirut on 3 September. At Beirut Djenne embarked 1644 passengers and Koutoubia embarked 1515 passengers. Total number of passengers was 3159 of which 2635 were military personnel. The convoy departed Beirut on 4 September and arrived at Marseille on 10 September. (13)
4 Sep 1941 Around 0650 B/4, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) ended her 9th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (13)

16 Sep 1941 At 1510 B/16, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Malta for her 10th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the approaches to Tripoli, Libya to intercept an important enemy troop convoy together with HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, DSO, RN), HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) and HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wright, DSC, RN).
HMS Unbeaten was to patrol in position 32°52'N, 15°03'E by 0001 B/18 and in position 33°15'N, 14°43'E by 2100 B/18.

The convoy that was to be intercepted was made up of the Italian troop transports Neptunia (19475 GRT, built 1932), Oceania (19507 GRT, built 1933) and Vulcania (24469 GRT, built 1928). They were escorted by the destroyers Nicoloso da Recco, Antonio da Noli, Antoniotto Usodimare, Emanuele Pessagno, Vincenzo Gioberti and Alfredo Oriani. This convoy was to proceed from Taranto to Tripoli.
18 Sep 1941 At 0020 B/18, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) arrived in her patrol position (32°52'N, 15°03'E). She commenced to patrol at slow speed in a 150°-330° direction.

At 0307 B/18, a vessel was sighted bearing 010° at a distance of eight miles. HMS Unbeaten immediately altered course to 360° and closed at full speed, but the range was obviously far too great to get in an attack as the vessel could only just be seen as a small dark smudge though binoculars.
At 0311 B/18, a second shape was visibile on the same bearing and range. No destroyers were sighted.
At 0312 B/18, HMS Unbeaten transmitted an enemy report by SS/T to HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, DSO, RN).
At 0313 B/18, it was attempted to pass an enemy reported by W/T but the relay switch was found to be defective. This was repaired by 0324 hours and the report was finally passed at 0340 B/18.
At 0355 B/18, HMS Unbeaten turned to follow in the wake of the convoy in case any disabled ships may be encountered.
At 0501 B/18, HMS Upholder's 0419 B/18 was received and speed was increased to maximum to intercept the damaged ships reported.
From that time distant explosions were almost continuous until 0930 hours. Some 200 depth charges were counted.
At 0603 B/18, Unbeaten dived.
At 0652 B/18, masts and the funnel of a large merchant vessel were sighted on bearing 342°. Unbeaten closed at speed.
At 0700 B/18, the vessel was observed to be stopped.
At 0725 B/18, the range to the vessel was now 12000 yards. It was identified as an Oceania-class liner.
At 0728 B/18, smoke and two masts were sighted on bearing 313°. This appeared to be another large merchant vessel but it was not seen again.
At 0740 B/18, five destroyers were observed to approach from the westward and one destroyer appeared to be alongside the target or at least very close to the target.

At 0840 B/18, a destroyer forced HMS Unbeaten to go deep. A very loud explosion was heard nine minutes later followed by a second very cloud explosion a minute after the first. It was thought Unbeaten had been detected and was being depth charged.

At 0858 B/18, a lound and uncanny rending noise was heard very close. This was at first thought to be a sweep wire, as a destroyer could be heard almost overhead, but as nothing further occurred it was assumed to be the target breaking up.
At 0919 B/18, HMS Unbeaten was back at periscope depth. This revealed a destroyer only 200 yards away and no sign of the target.

At 0926 B/18, a good look round showed four destroyers but no target. An enemy aircraft was seen patrolling in the area. Lt.Cdr. Woodward decided to clear the area and course was set to the second ordered position (33°15'N, 14°43'E).
Between 1200 B/18 and 1300 B/18, a number of distant explosions were heard.

At 1300 B/18, four destroyers and one C.A.N.T. seaplane were observed still in the vicinity of the targets position. It was assumed they were still searching for the submarine and / or picking up survivors.
At 1915 B/18, HMS Unbeaten arrived in position 33°15'N, 14°43'E.

At 2323 B/18, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2140 B/18 was received which ordered the four submarines involved in this convoy interception operation to return to Malta. Course was set accordingly. (24)
20 Sep 1941 At 0615 B/20, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) ended her 10th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (24)

23 Sep 1941 Around 1900 B/23, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) departed Malta for her 11th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the south coast of Calabria (area 'Y').
She was also to carry out a special operation (train wrecking) if the opportunity arose. For this purpose Lt. R.F. Broome, South Lanvashire Regt. and Corporal H. Slater were on board.
Lt. Norman had taken command of Unbeaten for this patrol in order to give Lt.Cdr. Woodward a rest.
25 Sep 1941 At 1130 B/25, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) was two miles from Cape Bruzzano where a periscope reconnaissance was carried out of the beaches and target for Folbot operations.
On completion of the reconnaissance course was set for Cape Spartivento. (24)
26 Sep 1941 At 0500 B/26, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) picked up diesel HE on bearing 300°. She turned towards to investigate.

At 0515 B/26, it was concluded that the source of the HE may be HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) which was patrolling in area 'X' to the west of HMS Unbeaten. [HMS Ursula reported nothing though around this time.]
At 1115 B/26, two minesweepers were sighted near Cape Spartivento. They passed to the westwards at 1325 B/26, 2000 yards to the north of HMS Unbeaten.
P.M., Lt. Broome was sick with sandfly fever. He was not fit for Folbot operations. (24)
27 Sep 1941 At 1130 B/27, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) was passed by two minesweepers proceeding eastwards. They later returned and passed HMS Unbeaten westbound at 1300 B/26.
At 1245 B/27, in approximate position 37°54'N, 15°50'E, HMS Unbeaten sighted smoke off Cape Spartivento.

At 1310 B/27, the smoke was identified as coming from three small coastal stramers of 800 to 1500 tons escorted by a Generali-class torpedo boat. Dived that this convoy was not worth attacking and thereby compromising the position of the submarine as enemy Fleet units were expected in the future.

This was most probable the convoy made up of the Italian Docilitas (1246 GRT, built 1900), Maralunga (557 GRT, built 1921) and the Vichy French Oued Yquem (Italian, 1326 GRT, built 1920) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Rosolino Pilo which sailed from Crotone at 6 knots at 1845 B/26 and arrived at Messina at 1810 B/27.

At 1630 B/27, masts and a funnel were sighted off Cape Spartivento. Unbeaten closed to investigate and ten minutes later the contact was identified as the Italian naval tanker Prometeo (1200 tons, built 1920). She was not attacked for the same reasons as given above with the escorted convoy of small merchant vessels.
[We have not been able to identify this vessel.]
At 1820 B/27, smoke was sighted to the southwards moving towards Messina. HMS Unbeaten turned to the westward and proceeded at high speed to intercept.
At 1838 B/27, it was seen that the target was a large ship of about 10000 tons. Range was 6 miles. An attack was commenced.
At 1858 B/27, the range to the target was 8000 yards. Distance off track was 3000 yards.
At 1859 B/27, the target was identified as the hospital ship, most likely the Sicilia (9646 GRT, built 1924) and the attack was broken off. Position was 235° - Cape Spartivento - 11.5 miles.

This was either Virgilio (Italian 11718 GRT, built 1926) which sailed from Augusta at 1045 B/27 for Palermo or Arno (Italian, 8024 GRT, built 1912) which sailed from Benghazi at 1500 B/26 and arrived at Naples at 0915 B/28. Most probably the latter.
At 2155 B/27, turbine HE was picked up, 170 revs, on bearing 045°. Unbeaten, which was running on the surface, turned towards and the generators were stopped. Visibility was 1.5 miles.
At 2207 B/27, a gunflash was seen fairly close to the northward. Unbeaten dived.
At 2216 B/27, destroyer HE was heard passing from east to west. It faded out to the north-west at 2230 B/27.
The HE picked up may possibly have been from the Italian torpedo boat San Martino which sailed at 1816 B/27 for an A/S sweep and returned to Messina at 1000 B/28. (24)
28 Sep 1941 At 0107 B/28, in approximate position 37°45'N, 15°50'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) picked up destroyer HE bearing 270°. The generators were stopped and Unbeaten turned towards.
At 0136 B/28, the HE faded out.

At 0230 B/28, loud HE was picked up on bearing 250°. Thought to be probably a motor torpedo boat. Visibility was 3 to 4 miles. Nothing was in sight. Lt. Norman decided to carry out a surface Asdic watch with the generators stopped as light enemy surface force activity possibly heralded the arrival of the enemy Battle Fleet.
At 0330 B/28, destroyer HE was picked up on bearing 310°. Also a dim light was seen from the same direction.
At 0343 B/28, flicker light was seen on the same bearing as HE from a motor torpedo boat was picked up.
At 0525 B/28, in position 200° - Cape Spartivento - 14 miles. HMS Unbeaten dived for submerged patrol. No HE was heard after diving, all was clear.
At 0700 B/28, Lt. Norman decided to patrol in the vicinity of last lights enemy activity as it was considered to be the likely point of approach to Messina for the enemy Battle Fleet.
At 0738 B/28, while in approximate position 37°45'N, 15°56'E, a Confienza-class torpedo boat was seen proceeding westwards towards Cape dell'Armi on course 300°.

This may have been the Italian torpedo boat San Martino which sailed at 1816 B/27 for an A/S sweep and returned to Messina at 1000 B/28. She may also have been responsible for (some of) the HE that was picked up during the night.
At 2002 B/28, HMS Unbeaten surfaced in position 190° - Cape Spartivento - 9 miles. Nothing further had been sighted all day except for an enemy aircraft at 0925 B/28. (24)

29 Sep 1941 At 1120 B/29, while in approximate position 37°51'N, 15°58'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) sighted two minesweepers passing proceeding eastwards. They passed again proceeding westwards at 1315 B/29.
At 1140 B/29, smoke was sighted on bearing 192°. HMS Unbeaten turned to the south-west to intercept.
At 1148 B/29, the source of the smoke was identified as the Vichy-French hospital ship Canada (9684 GRT, built 1912).
At 2002 B/29, HMS Unbeaten surfaced in position 175° - Cape Spartivento - 8 miles. Nothing further had been sighted all day. Lt. Broome was still not fit enough to carry out a Folbot operation. (24)
30 Sep 1941 At 0500 B/30, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) dived in position 145° - Cape dell'Armi - 8 miles.
At 1004 B/30, minesweepers passed proceeding eastwards. At 1322 B/30 they passed again but now westwards.
Nothing further, except several aircraft, were sighted during the day.

At 2006 B/30, HMS Unbeaten surfaced in position 37°47'N, 16°05'E. Lt. Broome was still not fit for Folbot operations, besides that the swell had got up. Lt. Norman decided that the only possible day left to carry out a Folbot operation was be the next night.
The next day weather conditions were however unsuitable and the attempt to carry out a Folbot operation on the enemy coast was abandoned. (24)
3 Oct 1941 At 0515 B/3, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) dived in position 195° - Cape Spartivento - 12 miles.
At 1500 B/3, HMS Unbeaten began to retire to seaward.
Between 1515 B/3 and 1525 B/3, three moderately close explosions were heard.
At 1630 B/3, HE was picked up on bearing 115°. HMS Unbeaten came to periscope depth to investigate.
At 1637 B/3, a Curtatone-class torpedo boat was sighted on bearing 155° at a range of only 500 yards and coming towards. Lt. Norman immediately took HMS Unbeaten deep.
This was most probably the Italian torpedo boat San Martino which sailed from Messina at 1810 B/2 for an A/S sweep and returned to Messina at 1918 B/3.
The enemy passed astern at only 300 yards a minute later.
At 1650 B/3, the HE faded and on coming back to periscope depth noting was sighted.
At 2105 B/3, HMS Unbeaten surfaced in position 177° - Cape Spartivento - 11 miles. (24)
4 Oct 1941 At 0515 B/4, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) dived in position 38°07'N, 16°19'E.
At 1025 B/4, she was in position 090° - Cape Bruzzano - 1000 yards.
At 1032 B/4, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) surfaced to bombard a railway.

At 1039 B/4, the bombardment completed, HMS Unbeaten dived. Twenty rounds had been fired, also at a Home Guard hut near the railway. The results were unclear but later two trains were seen stopped outside the tunnel that had been bombarded.
Italian sources recorded that thirteen rounds had landed in the area but they caused no damage.
During the day it was obvious that an air search was ongoing in the area so HMS Unbeaten remained deep for most of the day.
At 1950 B/4, HMS Unbeaten surfaced in position 37°44'N, 16°09'E and then set course to return to Malta. (24)
6 Oct 1941 At 0700 B/6, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) ended her 11th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (24)

14 Oct 1941 Around 2130 A/14, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Malta for her 12th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the south-east coast of Sicily, in position 126° - Cape Murro di Porco - 17.5 miles, to intercept three Italian destroyers of the Navigatori-class there are thought to make a high speed run to North Africa with supplies.

This referred to the destroyers Emanuele Pessagno (310 troops), Antonio Da Noli (295 troops) and Nicolò Zeno (268 troops) which sailed from Augusta at 1830 B/15 - 1850 B/15. They were transporting German troops to Benghazi where they arrived at 1630 B/16 - 2030 B/16.

15 Oct 1941 At 0930 A/15, east of Cape Passero, Sicily, in position 36°40'N, 15°34'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted a hospital ship steering 185°. This was identified as being the Virgilio (Italian, 11718 GRT, built 1928). This was indeed correct as the Virgilio had sailed at 0730 B/15 from Augusta bound for Tripoli where she arrived at 1015 B/16.
At 2200 A/15, HMS Unbeaten left patrol and set course to return to Malta. The three Italian troop transporting destroyers had not been sighted. (24)
16 Oct 1941 Around 0915 A/16, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) ended her 12th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (24)
