19 Oct 1941 Around 1800 A/19, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Malta for her 13th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the east coast of Sicily between Cape Passero and Messina.
She was to proceed through position 36°00'N, 14°53'E and 35°51'N, 15°15'E en-route to her patrol area.

20 Oct 1941 In a signal timed 1041 A/20, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) and HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN); If possible establish patrols as follows from dusk tonight 20/10. HMS Unbeaten in position 073° - Cape Murro di Porco - 7.5 miles. HMS Ursula in position 118° - Cape Murro di Proco - 11.5 miles. Large destroyers might pass to the south-east during night. After attack or at 0430 A/21 proceed in execution of previous orders.
This referred to a troop transport run by the Italian destroyers Antonio da Noli, Nicolò Zeno and Emanuele Pessagno from Augusta to Benghazi.

They were transporting German troops; Antonio da Noli: 9 officers and 258 other ranks. Nicolò Zeno: 7 officers and 263 other ranks. Emanuele Pessagno: 4 officers and 262 other ranks. They sailed from Augusta at 1950 B/20-2025 B/20 and arrived at Benghazi at 1600 B/21-1840 B/21. (25)

20 Oct 1941 At 2005 A/20, while to the east of Cape Murro di Proco, Sicily, in position 37°01'N, 15°40'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) heard HE on bearing 335°. Course was altered and speed increased to close to attack.

At 2015 A/20, a motor torpedo boat was sighted on bearing 320° apparently moving slowly on an easterly course. HMS Unbeaten dived. The enemy continued on it's course to the eastward and was seen or heard again. (24)

21 Oct 1941 At 1951 A/21, while to the east of Catania, Sicily, in position 37°30'N, 15°25'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted a small destroyer bearing 320° about half a mile away. An attack was not possible as HMS Unbeaten was only about 15° on the destroyers starboard bow. HMS Unbeaten dived to avoid being sighted.

This destroyer remained in the vicinity most of the night, apparently hunting as her H.E. could be heard intermittently, although she was not sighted again in spite of the fact that HMS Unbeaten closed on the surface on each occasion of hearing the enemy's HE.
At 0345 A/22, the last of the destroyer's HE died away to the north-westward and nothing more was heard or seen from this enemy destroyer.

An outside possibility is the Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori which had sailed from Messina at 1745 B/21 for an A/S sweep and returned at 1055 B/22. But to be in this position, she would have to travel at over 23 knots, which is doubtful. (24)

22 Oct 1941 At 0922 A/22, while to the east of Catania, Sicily, in position 37°30'N, 15°15'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted two Italian RD-class minesweepers passing 500 yards to the westward. They were minesweeping when sighted which confirmed the position of the Italian swept channel to Catania.

At 2240 A/22, in position 37°13'N, 15°44'E, a destroyer was sighted bearing 175° at a range of 3 miles. HMS Unbeaten closed to attack but the destroyer, apparently at the end of his A/S sweep, turned 180° and disappeared to the southward.
[We have been unable to identify the vessels sighted. We could not find evidence of a destroyer or torpedo boat operating in this general area on this day.] (24)

24 Oct 1941 At 1050 A/24, east of Taormina, Sicily, in approximate position 37°51'N, 15°25'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted an enemy Abba-class torpedo boat which appeared to be on an A/S patrol. Course was altered to the south to evade.
This was the Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza which sailed at 1100 B/24 from Messina and arrived at Augusta at 1450 B/24. (26)

25 Oct 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) made a survey of the coast in the vicinity of Taormina to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1545 A/24 in which he asked for coastal information.
A proposed bombardment of a train in Taormina station was not carried out, as a gun emplacement was located in position 304° - Cape Taormina - 5.8 cables.
No other suitable place for train bombardment was observed, nor was any shipping, other than the usual smattering of open fishing boats sighted.

At 1243 A/25, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1243 A/25 was received which gave the following orders; Established patrol in the vicinity of position 37°02'N, 15°29'E in anticipation of similar operation as in my signal timed 1041 A/20. Enemy forces may return to Messina in the afternoon of 27th. Course was set to comply with these orders.

It was known from ULTRA intercepts that the destroyers Folgore, Sebenico, Nicoloso da Recco, Alfredo Oriani, Vincenzo Gioberti and Antoniotto Usodimare were at Tripoli (see DEFE3/833/page 462, 395, 367, etc.). They had been in Tripoli for several days (Sebenico since 14/10, the others since 19/10) and sailing every evening to spend the night outside the harbour, in case of air raids. Perhaps someone guessed that their departure for Italy was imminent. In fact, an intercept on the following day, indicated that Nicoloso da Recco, Antoniotto Usodimare, Alfredo Oriani and Vincenzo Gioberti would leave for Trapani at 2000 B/26 at 20 knots (DEFE3/833/p. 367). (27)
26 Oct 1941 At 0700 A/26, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) arrived in the patrol position as ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1243 A/25. Nothing was sighted all day. (24)

27 Oct 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) attacked an Italian submarine about 25 nautical miles south-east of Augusta, Sicily, Italy In position 36°54'N, 15°36'E. Four torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained.
According to Italian sources this was almost certainly Topazio returning to Augusta from a patrol in the Eastern Mediterranean but she did not observe the attack.
0633 A/27 - In position 36°54'N, 15°36'E sighted an Italian Gemma-class submarine on the surface bearing 205°, range 6000 yards, speed 12 knots, enemy course 340°. Started attack.
0643 A/27 - Fired four torpedoes from 3500 yards. All torpedoes missed. (24)

28 Oct 1941 At 1600 A/28, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1047 A/26 in which she had been ordered to arrive off Malta at 0630 A/30. She was to proceed through positions 35°51'N, 15°15'E and 36°00'N, 14°53'E on the way back. (27)
30 Oct 1941 Around 0645 A/30, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) ended her 13th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (24)
1 Nov 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) was docked in No.1 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (28)
11 Nov 1941 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) is undocked. (28)

15 Nov 1941 Around 1730 B/15, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Malta for 14th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol east off Tripoli, Libya in area 'D', between Latitudes 32°00'N and 32°45'N and Longitudes 15°00'E and 15°53'E.

16 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1051 B/16 [another source says 1021 B/16] the following orders were sent by Capt. S.1 to HMS Perseus (Lt.Cdr. E.C.F. Nicolay, DSO, RN) and HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN); HMS Perseus is to establish patrol forthwith in the vicinity of position 35°16'N, 19°26'E. HMS Unbeaten is to establish patrol in the vicinity of position 33°04'N, 15°30'E by 0001 B/19. Operational sanctuary is established within 25 miles of these positions from 1201 B/17 for HMS Perseus and from 2000 B/18 for HMS Unbeaten. (29)

18 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1545 B/18, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) and HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN); HMS Ursula, leave patrol forthwith and return to Malta passing through positions 33°00'N, 14°50'E and 35°20'N, 14°14'E thence as ordered by Capt. S.10. HMS Unbeaten, remain in present position until 0200 B/19 thence to area 'D l', but to be in the vicinity of position 33°05'N, 14°34'E by 1200 B/20. If nothing sighted by 2200 B/20 resume patrol in area 'D 1'. (29)

19 Nov 1941 At 0156 B/19, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2207 B/19 ordering her to establish patrol off Tripoli in the vicinity of position 33°06'N, 12°58'E. Course was set accordingly and HMS Unbeaten arrived in the position ordered around 0800 B/20. (24)

21 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 0902 B/21, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) to patrol in position 33°16'N, 12°51'E, unless otherwise ordered by Capt. S.1. An enemy ship proceeding to Tripoli was expected to proceed through that position on course 155°.

We could not find any ULTRA decrypt to match this, so perhaps it is lost. This may have referred to the Italian transport Fabio Filzi (6836 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyers Antoniotto Usodimare and Saetta which sailed from Trapani at 1817 B/22-1840 B/22 from Trapani for Tripoli, averaging 17 knots. They were reinforced by the destroyer Sebenico which sailed from Tripoli at 0030 B/23 and joined them at 0755 B/23, and then piloted to Tripoli by the torpedo boat Centauro which had departed from there at 1200 B/23 and met them at 1320 B/23. They arrived at their destination at 1400 B/23-1612 B/23.
At 0917 B/21, in position 32°54'N, 12°47'E, HMS Unbeaten sighted a Freccia-class destroyer proceeding on course 085° at 18 knots.
This was the Italian torpedo boat Calliope which sailed from Zuara at 0720 B/21 and arrived at Tripoli at 1200 B/21. (24)

22 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 0015 B/22, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) to patrol in position 32°55'N, 14°20'E, unless otherwise ordered by Capt. S.1. She was to proceed through position 33°15'N, 13°35'E en-route to this new patrol position. A convoy was to be expected on an approach course of 260° in the afternoon of 23 November. HMS Unbeaten arrived in the ordered position around 0600 B/23.

This must have been to intercept an important enemy convoy which turned back to Italy after the heavy cruiser Trieste of the cover force was torpedoed by HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) and the convoy itself had been attacked by Allied aircraft. (27)

23 Nov 1941 By 1930 B/23, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) had seen nothing of the expected convoy. Lt. Woodward decided to act on the information in Capt. S.1's signal timed 2025 B/22 which contained the following information; Two merchant ships escorted by two torpedo boats in position 31°34'N, 18°44'E at 0900 B/22 proceeding on course 265° at 7 knots.
HMS Unbeaten set course to close the coast and wait for the convoy to come her way.

The signal referred to the convoy made up of the German transport Brook (1225 GRT, built 1927) and the Italian Amba Aradam (405 GRT, built 1932) escorted by the submarine chasers Selve and Arcione which were reported by an ULTRA intercept to have sailed from Benghazi at 1800 B/21 at 7 knots for Tripoli and would be off Buerat at dawn on 23/11 (DEFE3/833/p. 688). They actually arrived at Buerat in the evening of 22/11. The torpedo boat Generale Antonino Cascino sailed from Tripoli at 1920 B/22 and arrived at Buerat at 0745 B/23 and sailed again with the two merchant ships at 0815 B/23 and arrived at Tripoli at 1000 B/24. The two submarine chasers had sailed back to Benghazi the previous night.
At 2000 B/23, HMS Unbeaten was north-west of Khoms, in position 32°47'N, 14°11'E. Both engines were stopped and A/S listening watch was set. HMS Unbeaten was only 4 miles from the coast.

At 2109 B/23, the Vice Admiral Malta's signal timed 1734 B/23 was received which stated the following; At 1500 B/23 two destroyers escorting a large transport were sighted in position 32°15'N, 15°30'E. Enemy course 340°, speed unknown.
As it was thought these ships were proceeding to Tripoli they would keep close to the coast. HMS Unbeaten thus remained in position, close inshore.
Possibly there was an error in the signal and it was two merchantmen and a destroyer and must have referred again to convoy specified above. No other vessels were in this area at that time. (27)

24 Nov 1941 At 0105 B/24, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) proceeded northwards as Lt.Cdr. Woodward assumed that the important convoy mentioned in Capt. S.10's signal timed 0015 B/22 had been delayed and might attempt to make Tripoli at dawn on this day, he decided to return to the originally ordered position 16 miles north of Khoms to intercept it. Any slow coast crawling traffic making Tripoli at dawn, and the merchant vessel and destroyer reported in the Vice Admiral Malta's signal timed 1734 B/24, should have passed HMS Unbeaten by now.
At 0645 B/24, HMS Unbeaten returned to her inshore patrol position. There had been no sign of the important convoy.
In the same signal she was ordered to leave patrol P.M. on the 26th to arrive at Malta at 0730 B/28 proceeding through positions 33°00'N, 15°00'E, 34°10'N, 14°30'E and 35°34'N, 14°10'E en-route.

At 1153 B/24, HMS Unbeaten received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1011 B/24 ordering her to patrol in area 'D 1'. Lt.Cdr. Woodward decided to proceed eastwards along to coast, also inspecting Khoms roads for shipping.
At 1527 B/24, an RD-class minesweeper was sighted proceeding westwards very close inshore past Khoms. No other shipping was seen off Khoms.
At 1752 B/24, HMS Unbeaten turned to the north-eastward to open from the coast for the night and proceed to area 'D 1'. (24)
25 Nov 1941 At 0100 B/25, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) entered area 'D 1' and proceed to the southward to make the coast to the south of Misurata.
At 1427 B/25, in position 32°11'N, 15°26'E, HMS Unbeaten sighted two trawlers and a Generali-class torpedo boat conducting an A/S sweep south along the coast. Avoiding action was taken.
A small explosive charge was heard every 10 minutes. Also the grumbling of the sweep wires could be heard.

These were the steamers Priaruggia (Italian, 1196 GRT, built 1925) and Ascianghi (Italian, 610 GRT, built 1921) escorted by the torpedo boat Generale Antonio Cantore which had sailed from Tripoli at about 7 knots at 1700 B/24 for Benghazi. At 1200 B/25, they were off Misurata. They arrived at Benghazi at 1135 B/27. (24)

26 Nov 1941 At 0053 B/26, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2341 B/25 ordering her to remain in the vicinity of position 32°35'N, 15°20'E until 1900 B/26. A fast (14.5 knot) westbound merchant ship and an escorting destroyer were to be expected.
At 0500 B/26, HMS Unbeaten arrived in the position ordered.

The ships mentioned in the signal referred to the the motorship Città di Tunisi (Italian, 5419 GRT, built 1929), carrying PoW's, escorted by the destroyer Lanzerotto Malocello. They had sailed from Benghazi at 1705 B/25 for Tripoli. At 1519 B/26, 7 miles north of Ras Hallab, they were attacked by two low-flying (40-50 metres) RAF bombers, each dropping eight bombs on the merchant vessel. There were no direct hits, but Città di Tunisia signalled that she required a tow. However, the destroyer noticed that she had a hole, two metres above the waterline, and the towing was judged unnecessary. They arrived at Tripoli at 2100 B/26.
At 2000 B/26, HMS Unbeaten left area 'D 1' and course was set to return to Malta. (24)
28 Nov 1941 Around 0730 B/28, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (24)

7 Dec 1941 Around 1800 B/7, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol south of the Straits of Messina in area F which was bounded by the following positions; 37°58'N, 15°33'E, 37°03'N, 15°45'E, 37°07'N, 16°10'E and 38°02'N, 15°28'E.

HMS Unbeaten was part of a patrol line together with HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN). HMS Upright was operating to the west of HMS Unbeaten while HMS Unique was to the east.

9 Dec 1941 At 0530 B/9, in position 37°42'N, 15°49'E, HE was heard by HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) and nine minutes later three ships were seen which were thought to be Freccia-class destroyers coming directly at the submarine. Torpedoes had been readied but the short range meant that they might pass under and the attack abandoned.
These were most probably Granatiere, Alpino, Fuciliere and Bersagliere on passage from Taranto to Naples.
They had sailed from Taranto at 18 knots at 1600 B/8-1609 B/8 and arrived at Naples at 1805 B/9-1900 B/9.

At 0811 B/9, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0413 B/9 was received with ordered HMS Unbeaten to patrol at the entrence to the Gulf of Taranto in position 39°42'N, 17°58'E. Course was set to comply with these orders.

At 2332 B/9, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1654 B/9 was received adjusting the patrol position of HMS Unbeaten to 39°40'N, 17°52'E. HMS Unbeaten was part of a patrol line which was further to be made up of HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN). (24)

9 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1928 B/9, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) were ordered that no northbound merchant ships of more than 2000 tons and less than 10000 tons whether escorted or not is to be attacked by you from receipt of this message until noon 11/l2 unless it is definitely identified as a tanker.
The above signal was cancelled in Capt. S.10's signal timed 0104 B/10. (30)
11 Dec 1941 At 1550 B/11, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) was in position 39°35'N, 17°51'E. Between 1630 B/11 and 1645 B/11, a number of explosions were heard.
At 1800 B/11, she arrived in the position ordered, 39°40'N, 17°52'E. (24)

11 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 0924 B/11, Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) to form R line as follows: 3 - 39°27'N, 17°24'E - 060° - 13 - 150° - 20. Position A (39°27'N, 17°24'E), HMS Upright, Position B, HMS Utmost and position C, HMS Unbeaten. (30)
12 Dec 1941 At 0300 B/12, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) picked up HE on bearing 345°. Course was altered towards and speed was increased to close.

At 0318 B/12, the contact was identified as a small class destroyer (Spica-class torpedo boat) proceeding westwards. She appeared to be carrying out an A/S patrol. HMS Unbeaten dived to avoid being sighted. She surfaced again at 0335 B/12.
This was most probably the Italian torpedo boat Lince which sailed from Taranto at 2240 B/11 for an A/S sweep and returned at 1527 B/12.
At 1205 B/12, HMS Unbeaten set course to position 40°12'N, 17°19'E as ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1034 B/12.
By midnight, Lt.Cdr. Woodward had taken HMS Unbeaten a little closer to Taranto that had been ordered in the hope of sighting minesweepers at dawn and so establishing the swept channel. (24)

12 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1034 B/12, Capt. S.10 gave the following orders to HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN);

Proceed forthwith to establish patrol during daylight hours on 13 December as follows: Upright in position 40°08'N, 17°00'E, Utmost in position 40°00'N, 17°11'E, Unbeaten in position 40°12'N, 17°19'E. Attention is called to QB 4 (an Italian minefield) Heavy units may leave Taranto A.M./13 proceeding south. After attack or at 1930 B/13 withdraw to original patrol as in my signal 0924 B/11.

In a signal timed 1615 B/12, the Capt. S.10 infored the above submarines that two merchant vessels and three destroyers had been spotted at 1505 B/12 in position 38°00'N, 17°00'E proceeding on course 030° at 10 knots. (30)

13 Dec 1941 At 0145 B/13, the leading light of Taranto were switched on bearing 013° and 040°. This put HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) only 6 miles bearing 205° from the Taranto breakwater. At the same time HE was heard on bearing 155°. The sea was calm and the moon was up. Unbeaten proceeded at speed to get down moon and close the source of the HE.
At 0200 B/13, a dark object was sighted which was believed to be a motor torpedo boat bearing 030° at a range of about 2 miles. Unbeaten dived to avoid being sighted.
At 0208 B/13, depth charges were heard to explode. Some appeared quite close. These charges continued to be dropped throughout the whole night.
At 0226 B/13, HE was heard on bearing 250° which might have been a merchant vessel. Lt.Cdr. Woodward decided to surface and investigate.
At 0229 B/13, a very loud explosion was heard and HE was heard to be closing.
At 0232 B/13, HMS Unbeaten surfaced and closed the HE at speed.
At 0259 B/13, two vessels were sighted on bearing 230°. Unbeaten closed to investigate.

At 0312 B/13, the vessels were identified as destroyers so Unbeaten dived. It appeared that the enemy were carrying out a systematic search and a large number of depth charges were being dropped. It was thought either HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) had fired at a target and that the enemy was hunting. It was also considered possible that HMS Unbeaten had crossed an indicator loop. Lt.Cdr. Woodward decided to retire from the area by proceeding submerged to the south-eastward.
The vessels sighted were probably the destroyers Nicoloso da Recco and Antoniotto Usodimare hunting HMS Upright after the sinking of Fabio Filzi and Carlo del Greco.
At 0622 B/13, four destroyers could be heard on bearings 150°, 180°, 085° and 240°.
At 0720 B/13, three destroyers were sighted a dawn broke. The enemy were 1.5 miles away to the south-eastward apparently hunting. HMS Unbeaten was now in position 40°16'N, 17°10'E.

This were again probably Nicoloso da Recco and Antoniotto Usodimare. Also the torpedo boat Sagittario and the MAS 5 D sailed from Taranto at 0443 B/13 to search for the survivors of the two ships and hunt the submarine.

At 0749 B/13, in position 40°16'N, 17°11'E, two minesweepers were sighted on course 193°. This established the swept channel as shown on the track chart. Lt.Cdr. Woodward decided to remain close eastward of this swept channel in the hope of being in a good position for an attack on anything leaving Taranto.

This were probably the minesweepers RD 6 and RD 13 which had sailed from Taranto at 0540 B/13 to assist the transports Carlo del Greco and Fabio Filzi which had been torpedoed by HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN).
At 0807 B/13, a schoote of about 150 tons was sighted proceeding down the swept channel. This again confirmed it's position.
This was possibly the auxiliary schooner Sparviero (498 GRT, built 1915) which sailed from Taranto at 0540 B/13 to assist Carlo del Greco and Fabio Filzi which had been torpedoed.
At 0838 B/13, an aircraft was seen patrolling in the vicinity of the harbour. Aircraft remained patrolling in the area for most of the day.

At 0915 B/13, a patrol vessel, resembling a more modern Ostia-class minelayer, was observed patrolling approximately 120° - 300° about 6 miles to seaward. She passed within one mile from HMS Unbeaten at 0930 B/13 and 1315 B/13.
Between 1030 and 1100 B/13, explosions were heard.
At 1320 B/13, Lt.Cdr. Woodward decided to close the swept channel still closer so course was set accordingly.

At 1440 B/13, the masts of a merchant vessel were sighted. This vessel was closed at full speed but the nearest HMS Unbeaten got was 9000 yards when she was 130° on the vessels port bow. The escort consisted of two destroyers but these were too far away to identify the type. The wake of the merchant vessel was closed at speed in the hope that something else might follow her out. The merchant vessel was of about 5500 GRT with a single squat funnel and 'goal post' masts. Also, besides the two destroyers, two aircraft were escorting this merchant vessel.

This was the German transport Ankara (4,768 GRT, built 1937) screened by the destroyer Lanzerotto Malocello which had sailed from Taranto at 1045 B/13 for Benghazi, at about 12-12.5 knots, on course 135°. The destroyer Saetta sailed from Taranto at 1158 B/13 and joined them at 1355 B/13. At 0200 B/14, they were ordered back and arrived at Taranto at 2129 B/14.
At 1550 B/13, two trawlers and one other small escort vessel passed up the swept channel towards Taranto.
At 1900 B/13, HMS Unbeaten proceeded on the surface on course 135° to clear the area and return to the original patrol position as ordered by Capt. S.10 in his signal timed 1034 B/12.
At 2300 B/13, in position 40°00'N, 17°26'E, the sound of a motor torpedo boat was heard. HMS Unbeaten dived. Nothing was sighted through the periscope and no HE was picked up.
At 2340 B/13, HMS Unbeaten surfaced.
Movements of destroyers and torpedo boats off Taranto on 13/12;

The destroyers Nicoloso da Recco and Antoniotto Usodimare and the torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza (recalled shortly after sailing) sailed from Messina at 0959 B/12-1005 B/12, escorting the transports Carlo del Greco and Fabio Filzi (both sunk by HMS Upright) and arrived at Taranto at 1000 B/13-1035 B/13.
The torpedo boat Sagittario sailed from Taranto at 0443 B/13 and returned at 1835 B/13.
The destroyer Lanzerotto Malocello sailed from Taranto at 1045 B/13 and returned at 2129 B/14.
The destroyer Saetta sailed from Taranto at 1158 B/13 and returned at 1757 B/14.
The destroyer Maestrale sailed from Taranto at 1435 B/13 and returned at 0039 B/15.
The torpedo boat Pegaso sailed from Taranto at 1445 B/13 and returned at 1330 B/14.
The destroyer Corazziere sailed from Taranto at 1530 B/13 and returned at 2305 B/14.
The destroyer Granatiere sailed from Taranto at 1648 B/13 and returned at 2314 B/14.
The destroyer Antonio da Noli sailed from Taranto at 1700 B/13 and returned at 0300 B/14.
The destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare sailed from Taranto at 1700 B/13 and returned at 0635 B/14.
The destroyer Vincenzo Gioberti sailed from Taranto at 1715 B/13 and returned at 2330 B/14.
The destroyer Alfredo Oriani sailed from Taranto at 1845 B/13 and returned at 0135 B/15.
The destroyer Carabiniere sailed from Taranto at 1900 B/13 and returned at 0347 B/14.
The destroyer Geniere sailed from Taranto at 1917 B/13 and returned at 0235 B/14.
The destroyer Ugolino Vivaldi sailed from Taranto at 1920 B/13 and returned at 0024 B/14.
The destroyer Nicoloso da Recco sailed from Taranto at 1955 B/13 and returned at 0200 B/14. (24)

13 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 2037 B/13, Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) to remain in position ordered as in his signal timed 1034 B/12, until attack or 1000 B/14 then returning to the original patrol line as ordered in his signal timed 0924 B/11. Big game was to be expected. (30)

14 Dec 1941 At 0156 B/14, inside the Gulf of Taranto, to the south-south-east of Taranto, in position 40°08'N, 17°22'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted two small destroyers, most likely torpedo boats proceeding northwards. Range was 6000 yards.
We could not find two small torpedo boats which matches description (see below). Perhaps it were motor torpedo boats ?
At 0240 B/14, HMS Unbeaten was in the ordered position, 40°12'N, 17°19'E.

Nothing was sighted though and at 1000 B/14, she proceeded on course 135° to return the patrol position that had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2037 B/13 which referred to his earlier signal timed 0924 B/11.
At 1607 B/14, in position 39°56'N, 17°30'E, HE was picked up on bearing 168°. Unbeaten closed at speed but nothing was sighted except for a bit of smoke.
Movements of destroyers and torpedo boats off Taranto on 14/12;
The torpedo boat Lince sailed from Taranto at 0709 B/14 and returned at 2210 B/14.
The destroyer Antonio da Noli sailed from Taranto at 1200 B/14 and returned at 2300 B/14.
The destroyer Fuciliere sailed from Naples at 1630 B/13 and arrived at Taranto at 2340 B/14.
The destroyer Bersagliere sailed from Naples at 1630 B/13 and arrived at Taranto at 2340 B/14.
The destroyer Granatiere sailed from Naples at 1648 B/13 and arrived at Taranto at 2314 B/14.
The destroyer Carabiniere sailed from Taranto at 1900 B/13 and returned at 0347 B/14 and sailed again at 1215 B/14 and returned at 0015 B/15.
The torpedo boat Antares sailed from Navarino at 1300 B/13 and arrived at Taranto at 0915 B/14.
The destroyer Aviere sailed from Taranto at 1250 B/14 and returned at 2305 B/14.
The torpedo boat Aretusa sailed from Taranto at 1430 B/14 and arrived at Gallipoli at 2310 B/14.
The destroyer Pigafetta sailed from Taranto at 0600 B/15 and arrived at Benghazi at 0930 B/16. (24)

15 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1205 B/15, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN); Leave patrol so as to arrive at Malta A.M. on the 19th passing through positions 39°20'N, 18°00'E at 0001 B/17 and then through positions 38°47'N, 18°00'E and 35°51'N, 15°15'E.
The signal was received by HMS Unbeaten at 2120 B/15. (31)
Operation MF 1, passage of the British supply ship HMS Breconshire to Malta.

Around 2200 B/15 the British supply ship HMS Breconshire (9776 GRT, built 1939) departed Alexandria being escorted by HMS Naiad (Capt. M.A.H. Kelsey, DSC, RN flying the flag of Rear-Admiral P.L. Vian, DSO and 2 Bars, RN), HMS Euryalus (Capt. E.W. Bush, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Carlisle (Capt. D.M.L. Neame, DSO, RN), HMS Jervis (Capt. P.J. Mack, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Kimberley (Cdr. J.S.M. Richardson, DSO, RN), HMS Kingston (Lt.Cdr. P. Somerville, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Kipling (Cdr. A. St.Clair Ford, RN), HMAS Nizam (Lt.Cdr. M.J. Clark, RAN), HMS Havock (Lt. G.R.G. Watkins, DSC, RN) and HMS Decoy (Lt.Cdr. J.M. Alliston, RN). This last destroyer was also to proceed to Malta for repairs to her bow that had been damaged in a collision at Alexandria on 4 December. HMS Breconshire was carrying oil fuel for Malta.

At 1100/16 the Allied destroyers HMS Sikh (Cdr. G.H. Stokes, DSC, RN), HMS Maori (Cdr. R.E. Courage, DSO, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Legion (Cdr. R.F. Jessel, RN) and HrMs Isaac Sweers (Cdr. J. Houtsmuller, RNN) departed Malta. They joined up with the convoy at daylight on the 17th. During the day the convoy was attacked by enemy high level and torpedo bombers

These were followed at 1800 B/16 by ‘Force K’; HMS Aurora (Capt. W.G. Agnew, CB, RN), HMS Penelope (Capt. A.D. Nicholl, RN), HMS Lance (Lt.Cdr. R.W.F. Northcott, RN) and HMS Lively (Lt.Cdr. W.F.E. Hussey, DSC, RN).

At dark on the 16th HMS Carlisle, HMS Havock and HMS Kingston were detached to make a W/T diversion to the eastward at midnight of the night of 16/17 and then to proceed to Alexandria. They were later joined by HMS Griffin (Capt. H.St.L. Nicolson, DSO and Bar, RN).

Enemy heavy forces were reported at sea at 2230 B/16 by the submarines HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) in the Gulf of Taranto area. Neither submarine was able to attack. The Italians were at sea to cover an imported Axis convoy to North Africa.

From Taranto had departed the transports Monginevro (5324 GRT, built 1940), Napoli (6142 GRT, built 1941) and Vettor Pisani (6339 GRT, built 1939). They had a close escort of the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Antonio Da Noli, Nicoloso da Recco, Lanzerotto Malocello, Emanuele Pessagno, Nicolò Zeno. From Naples the German transport Ankara (4768 GRT, built 1937) departed on the same day. She had a close escort made up of the destroyer Saetta and the torpedo-boat Pegaso.

Cover was provided by two groups of warships. One group was made up of the battleship Caio Dulio, the light cruisers Emanuele Filiberto Duca D’Aosta, Muzio Attendolo, Raimondo Montecuccoli and the destroyers Aviere, Ascari and Camicia Nera. The other, and larger group, was made up of the battleships Littorio, Andrea Doria, Guilio Cesare, heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trento and the destroyers Granatiere, Bersagliere, Fuciliere, Alpino, Corazziere, Carabiniere, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Alfredo Oriani and Vincenzo Gioberti.

The enemy heavy forces were reported by reconnaissance aircraft at 0825 B/17 and again at 1525 B/17 when they were with their convoy and only about 60 nautical miles from the Allied convoy. Very few Allied aircraft were available for reconnaissance and shadowing was therefore not carried out at all. At 1745 B/17 the Allied convoy unexpectedly ran into the larger of the Italian cover forces. The Italian battleships opened fire but drew off to the northward when the Allied convoy escorts closed to attack. Contact was lost in the dark. When both forces made contact HMS Breconshire was detached with HMS Havock and HMS Decoy as escorts. They later made rendez-vous with ‘Force K’.
To reinforce the convoy HMS Neptune (Capt. R.C. O’Coner, RN), HMS Jaguar (Lt.Cdr. L.R.K. Tyrwhitt, DSC, RN) and HMS Kandahar (Cdr. W.G.A. Robson, DSO, DSC, RN) were sailed from Malta.

The original convoy escorts meanwhile retired to the eastward and then proceeded to the north of Benghazi to try to intercept the enemy convoy but as it was bound for Tripoli they made no contact. They therefore retired eastwards and arrived at Alexandria during the night of 18/19 December. HMS Kipling had been damaged by a near miss on the 17th. One rating had been killed during this attack.
HMS Breconshire and her escorts arrived safely at Malta in the afternoon of December 18th. (32)

16 Dec 1941 At 1715 B/16, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1537 B/16 which cancelled the recall signal timed 1205 B/15. HMS Unbeaten was now ordered to patrol off the Gulf of Taranto in position 39°17'N, 18°00'E. Course was set to comply and HMS Unbeaten arrived in the ordered position around 2000 B/16.
At 2243 B/16, HE was heard bearing 315°. HMS Unbeaten commenced to close at speed.

At 2304 B/16, in position 39°17'N. 18°00'E, sighted four destroyers. Visibility was very poor being misty and patchy. These destroyers were not sighted until they were just a little over a mile away. Unbeaten manoeuvred to try to pass through the screen undetected, put the port wing destroyer zigged straight towards and the submarine was forced to dive.
At 2310 B/16, this destroyer passed right overhead only two minutes after the vents had been opened.

At 2320 B/16, HE indicated several destroyers and one possible large unit. A second screen could be heard approaching from the northward. HMS Unbeaten surfaced. A destroyer screen could just be seen to the southward. A larger shape was also seen, this was thought to be a cruiser.

At 2344 B/16, three destroyers were sighted bearing 350° at a range of about 1.5 miles. One again HMS Unbeaten manoeuvred at full speed to get through undetected but the port wing destroyer came straight towards.
At 2350 B/16, HMS Unbeaten again had to dive. The destroyer passed overhead only one minute after the vents had been opened.

HE was continuous and all round for the next 15 minutes and an attempt was made to identify heavy a ship and fire on it by using the Asdic. Although one possible heavy ship was heard, it was not a certainty and so it was believed that the two waves of light craft were probably an advance guard ahead of the Battle Fleet.
At 0012 B/17, HMS Unbeaten surfaced. Nothing was in sight and the assumption that the enemy Battle Fleet may follow proved not to be correct.
At 0106 B/17, an enemy report was passed (Unbeaten's signal timed 2358 B/16).
Movements of destroyers and torpedo boats off Taranto on 16/12;
The torpedo boat Lince sailed from Taranto at 0709 B/16 and arrived at Crotone at 2400 B/16.
The torpedo boat Aretusa sailed from Taranto at 0730 B/16 and arrived at Gallipoli at 1613 B/16.
The destroyer Fuciliere sailed from Taranto at 0945 B/16 and returned at 1840 B/19.
The destroyer Alpino sailed from Taranto at 0946 B/16 and returned at 1830 B/19.
The destroyer Bersagliere sailed from Taranto at 1000 B/16 and returned at 2030 B/19.
The destroyer Lanzerotto Malocello sailed from Taranto at 1038 B/16 and arrived at Tripoli at 1313 B/19.
The destroyer Antonio da Noli sailed from Taranto at 1200 B/16 and arrived at Tripoli at 1500 B/19.
The destroyer Nicoloso da Recco sailed from Taranto at 1205 B/16 and arrived at Tripoli at 1035 B/19.
The destroyer Ugolino Vivaldi sailed from Taranto at 1220 B/16 and arrived at Tripoli at 0400 B/19 or 1015 B/19.
The torpedo boat Pegaso sailed from Taranto at 1245 B/16 and arrived at Benghazi at 1005 B/19.
The destroyer Saetta sailed from Taranto at 1300 B/16 and arrived at Benghazi at 0935 B/19.
The destroyer Camicia Nera sailed from Taranto at 1305 B/16 and returned at 2149 B/19.
The destroyer Ascari sailed from Taranto at 1315 B/16 and returned at 2250 B/19.
The destroyer Aviere sailed from Taranto at 1330 B/16 and returned at 2306 B/19.
The destroyers Emanuele Pessagno and Nicolò Zeno sailed from Taranto at 1443 B/16 and arrived at Tripoli at 1400 B/19.
The destroyer Alfredo Oriani sailed from Taranto at 1700 B/16 and arrived at Argostoli at 0240 B/20.
The destroyer Maestrale sailed from Taranto at 1700 B/16 and arrived at Navarino at 2053 B/19.
The destroyer Vincenzo Gioberti sailed from Taranto at 1707 B/16 and arrived at Navarino at 1113 B/20.
The destroyer Granatiere sailed from Taranto at 1716 B/16 and arrived at Navarino at 0942 B/22.
The destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare sailed from Taranto at 1730 B/16 and returned at 1845 B/19.
The destroyer Corazziere sailed from Taranto at 1745 B/16 and arrived at Argostoli at 0230 B/20.
The destroyer Carabiniere sailed from Taranto at 1800 B/16 and returned at 1805 B/19.
Explanation for First Battle of Sirte (from the Axis side):

A convoy consisting of the German transport Ankara (4768 GRT, built 1937) and the Italian transports Monginevro (5324 GRT, built 1939), Napoli (6142 GRT, built 1941) and Vettor Pisani (6339 GRT, built 1940) screened by the Italian destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Antonio da Noli, Nicoloso da Recco, Emanuele Pessagno, Nicolò Zeno, Lanzerotto Malocello, Saetta, and the Italian torpedo boat Pegaso sailed from Taranto at 1200 B/16-1443 B/16.

They were covered by three squadrons: 1) The battleship Duilio, the light cruisers Emanuele Filiberto Duca d’Aosta, Eugenio Montecuccoli and Muzio Attendolo (7th Cruiser Division), screened by the destroyers Aviere, Ascari and Camicia Nera (11th Destroyer Squadron). They sailed from Taranto at 1305 B/16-1526 B/16 and returned at 1744 B/19-0120 B/20.

2) The heavy cruisers Gorizia and Trento (Third Cruiser Division), screened by the destroyers Maestrale, Alfredo Oriani and Vincenzo Gioberti. They sailed from Taranto at 1700/16-1900 B/16. The two heavy cruisers returned to Taranto at 1206 B/19. Maestrale, and Vincenzo Gioberti who went to Navarino, arriving at 1153 B/19-2053 B/19 and Alfredo Oriani which went to Argostoli, arriving at 0240 B/20.

3) The battleships Littorio, Giulio Cesare and Andrea Doria, screened by the destroyers Granatiere, Fuciliere, Bersagliere, Alpino, Corazziere, Carabiniere and Antoniotto Usodimare. They sailed from Taranto at 1830 B/16-2021 B/16.
The Duilio squadron sighted the convoy at 1800 B/16 and assumed the direct escort.
At 0720 B/17, the Littorio and Gorizia squadrons joined.
At 1530 B/17, A Ro 43 seaplane catapulted from Littorio sighted a battleship, four cruisers and twelve destroyers in 33°56'N, 18°56'E, course 270°, 20 knots.

At 1754 B/17, Doria opened fire on the enemy followed a minute later by Littorio at 32,000 metres. At 1804 B/17, fire was checked as the enemy was hidden behind a smoke screen and action was broken off.
At 0934 B/18, the Duilio squadron briefly came within sight of the Littorio squadron.
At 1327 B/18, Duilio left the convoy and rejoined the Littorio squadron at 1521 B/18. The 7th Cruiser Division continued with the escort of the convoy.
At 1230 B/18, Ankara escorted by Saetta and Pegaso were detached from the convoy and proceeded to Benghazi where they arrived at 0935 B/19-1005 B/19.
At 1314 B/18, Littorio sighted on the horizon the transport Ankara escorted by two destroyers.

At 0550 B/18, the destroyers Granatiere and Corazziere collided in approximate position 33°45'N, 17°29'E. The Third Cruiser division with the destroyers Maestrale, Alfredo Oriani and Vincenzo Gioberti were sent to their assistance and joined at 0820 B/18. Corazziere escorted by Alfredo Oriani (which had briefly towed her until the cable broke) proceeded at 9 knots to Argostoli where they arrived at 0230 B/20-0240 B/20. Granatiere had lost her forward section until the bridge and had to be towed by Vincenzo Gioberti and escorted by Maestrale. The German tug Max Behrendt arrived to help, later reinforced by the destroyers Strale and then Scirocco and Geniere and they proceeded at 5 knots to Navarino where they arrived at 0942 B/22-1113 B/22 (except Geniere which arrived at Argostoli at 1015 B/22, and Scirocco and Strale which were detached to Patras - just before they arrived at Navarino – where they arrived at 1635 B/22 ).

At 1915 B/18, the Tripoli convoy split in three sections to enter the harbour in succession: 1) Vettor Pisani escorted by Ugolino Vivaldi and Emanuele Pessagno. 2) Monginevro escorted by Nicoloso da Recco and Lanzerotto Malocello. 3) Napoli escorted by Antonio da Noli and Nicolò Zeno.
At 2212 B/18, about 30 miles east of Tripoli, Napoli was damaged by a torpedo bomber.
At 2256 B/18, Napoli and Nicolò Zeno collided. The destroyer arrived at Tripoli by its own means, but Napoli needed the assistance of the tug Ferruccio which sailed from Tripoli at 0155 B/19.

Monginevro and Vettor Pisani escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Antonio da Noli, Nicoloso da Recco, Emanuele Pessagno, Nicolò Zeno and Lanzerotto Malocello arrived at Tripoli at 1015-1500 B/19. Napoli arrived at 1630 B/19.

At dusk on the 19th, the three light cruisers of the 7th Division were attacked by torpedo bombers between Cape Colonne and Cape Rizzuto but came out unscathed. They arrived at Taranto at 1744 B/19-0120 B/20. The four battleships screened by the destroyers Carabiniere, Fuciliere, Alpino and Bersagliere reached Taranto at 1800 B/19-2030 B/19. (24)

17 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1903 B/17, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) and ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki) to form a patrol line. HMS Unbeaten was to occupy the central position in 39°16'N, 17°43'E with the other two submarines on a line of 060°, 10 miles apart. (30)
18 Dec 1941 At 0245 B/18, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) arrived in the position ordered by Capt. S.10 in his signal timed 1903 B/17, 39°16'N, 17°43'E. (24)
19 Dec 1941 At 0800 B/19, in position 39°11'N, 17°42'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted an Avieri-class destroyer which passed within 1000 yards of HMS Unbeaten.
At 0820 B/19, the destroyer was now at a range of 10000 yards. She turned 180° and disappeared to the northward.
This was most probably the Italian destroyer Geniere (see below).

At 1013 B/19, a Spica-class torpedo boat was sighted which patrolled in the vicinity until noon. This was considered a further indication that the enemy Battle Fleet was expected to pass close to Unbeaten's position.
This was most probable the Italian destroyer Lince (see below).

At 1200 B/19, in position 39°13'N, 17°42'E, smoke was sighted bearing 040°. HMS Unbeaten closed the smoke at full speed, but nothing more than this smoke was seen although HE was heard between 1211 and 1230 hours. This suggested a number of vessels proceeding to the northward. Visibility was very poor with heavy rain clouding the periscope badly so that high power observations were almost impossible.
Movements of destroyers and torpedo boats off Taranto on 19/12:
See the event for 16 December 1941 for the return time of the destroyers from the covering forces.
The torpedo boat Lince sailed from Taranto at 0000 B/19 and returned at 2110 B/19.
The destroyer Geniere sailed from Taranto at 0202 B/19 to assist the destroyer Granatiere damaged in a collision (see 16 December entry) and arrived at Argostoli at 1015 B/22. (24)
22 Dec 1941 At 0830 B/22, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) ended her 15th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (24)

4 Jan 1942 Around 1740 B/3, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Gulf of Taranto in position 39°05'N, 17°30'E, in a patrol line which was further made up of HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN) and ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki, DSO).

5 Jan 1942 In a signal from Capt. S.10 timed 1215 B/5, HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) and ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki, DSO) were ordered to take up their positions as ordered in Capt. S.10's signal timed 1523 B/4 as soon as possible. (34)

6 Jan 1942 At 1400 B/6, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) was in position 38°42'N, 17°30'E which was 23 miles due south of the ordered position, thus covering the possible advance of the enemy from southward. Unbeaten continued to proceed northwards towards the ordered position.
At 2000 B/6, HMS Unbeaten arrived in her ordered position, 39°05'N, 17°30'E and commenced to patrol in the vicinity. (33)

7 Jan 1942 In a signal from Capt. S.10 timed 1131 B/7, HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) and ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki, DSO) received new orders. HMS Unbeaten was to remain in position X (39°05'N, 17°30'E). ORP Sokol and HMS Thrasher were to open out at 0001 B/8 to 12 and 24 miles, 060°, respectively from HMS Unbeaten. (34)

9 Jan 1942 At 0039 B/9, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1137 B/8, which ordered her to patrol in area 'J' to the south of the Straits of Messina. Area 'J' was enclosed by the following positions; 37°30'N, 13°39'E, 36°56'N, 15°24'E, 37°30'N, 16°41'E and 38°05'N, 16°16'E. Course was set accordingly. (33)
12 Jan 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) torpedoed and sank the German submarine U-374 south-west of Cape Spartivento, Calabria, Italy in position 37°50'N, 16°00'E.
1015 B/12 - Sighted a uboat bearing 080°. Range was 1800 yards. Started attack.
1023 B/12 - Fired four torpedoes from 1300 yards. Two hits were obtained.
1036 B/12 - One survivor was spotted.
1037 B/12 - Surfaced to pick up the survivor that turned out to be a Ordinary Seaman Hans Ploch.
1041 B/12 - Submerged and proceeded southward to clear the area. (33)
16 Jan 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) closed the Calabrian coast in the vicinity of position 37°45'N, 15°45'E as ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1411 B/13.
At 1310 B/16, in position 37°52'N, 15°45'E, HMS Unbeaten sighted a mast and a funnel. Closed at full speed to investigate.

At 1320 B/16, the ship was identified as a small tug proceeding eastwards very close inshore while towing a lighter. As the tug had a gun and was so close inshore Lt.Cdr. Woodward decided not to engage the enemy with gunfire and the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2156 B/15 forbade the expenditure of torpedoes on small targets.
At 1345 B/16, two small RD-class minesweepers passed down the swept channel to the westwards while sweeping.
[We have been unable to identify the vessels seen on this day.] (33)

17 Jan 1942 At 1551 B/17, in position 37°45'N, 15°42'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted a three-masted schooner, which passed 300 yards from the submarine on course 090° at 6 knots. She had two machine guns fitted but did not appear to show any signs of being a 'Q-ship'. (33)

18 Jan 1942 At 0928 B/18, in position 37°43'N, 15°35'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted a hospital ship thought to be the Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928). This was indeed the Virgilio which had sailed from Naples at 1630 B/17 and arrived in Augusta at 1305 B/18. (33)
19 Jan 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) made a torpedo attack on an escorted tanker in position 37°48'N, 15°50'E. No hits were obtained.

The target was the Italian tanker Rondine (6468 GRT, built 1924) which was in convoy with the Italian transport Rapido (5758 GRT, built 1919). They were escorted by the torpedo boat Giuseppe Cesare Abba and were on passage from Syracuse (sailed at 2226 B/18) to Taranto (arrived at 1132 B/20).
0534 B/19 - Heard HE on bearing 120°. Closed to investigate.
0552 B/19 - Sighted a small destroyer or a torpedo boat bearing 090° at a range of 2 miles. HMS Unbeaten dived to avoid detection. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.]
0825 B/19 - In position 37°48'N, 15°50'E, sighted the funnel of a merchant vessel bearing 340° at a range of 10000 yards. Closed at full speed to attack.

0841 B/19 - Range was now 7200 yards. Track angle 135°. Identified the vessel as the transport Rapido. She was in ballast. Decided not to attack at such long range and in such bad visibility but to continue to close as the HE of two other vessels was head. A two-engined bomber was seen to be the air escort.
0900 B/19 - Sighted a tanker of about 7000 to 8000 tons escorted by a Generale-class torpedo boat at a range of 6300 yards bearing 023°. Decided to attack the tanker.

0906 B/19 - Fired four torpedoes from 7000 yards. Two faint explosions were heard in the fore ends but nothing was heard in the control room. Unbeaten meanwhile had gone deep so the result could not be observed.
0919 B/19 - HE from the escort was intermittent which suggested that it was manoeuvring but not closing the attacker.
0924 B/19 - Returned to periscope depth but find nothing in sight.

0948 B/19 - Heard a very loud explosion which was believed to be the tanker blowing up. The periscope was up at the time and and all round look revealed nothing. This explosion was extremely heavy and unlike depth charges or bombs. Two aircraft were searching in the area so Lt.Cdr. Woodward decided to retire to the southward.

1930 B/19 - With no torpedoes left and having been ordered to leave patrol at 0001 B/20 anyway, Lt.Cdr. Woodward decided to proceed immediately to Malta. A signal timed 0420 B/20 was cleared to Capt. S.10 at 0500 B/20. (33)
20 Jan 1942 At 1700 B/20, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) ended her 16th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.

While entering harbour on the surface from the north-east, HMS Unbeaten was attacked by six enemy fighter aircraft in position 35°57'N, 14°35'E. Unbeaten submerged to evade the attack but suffered slight damage to the bridge casing from machine gun and cannon fire.
[No logs are available for HMS Unbeaten from February 1942 onwards. Details may thus be missing from February 1942 until her loss in November 1942.] (33)

2 Feb 1942 Around 2000 B/2, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) departed Malta for her 17th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Kerkennah, Tunisia in area 'K' bordered by positions 35°00'N, 11°30'E and 34°20'N, 12°42'E.
Lt. Martin had taken command of HMS Unbeaten in order to give Lt.Cdr. Woodward a rest.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (33)
4 Feb 1942 On this day HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) entered her patrol area (area 'K') in position 35°00'N, 11°35'E.
At 1430 B/4, a wreck was sighted on bearing 031°. Unbeaten closed but was unable to identify it.
At 2030 B/4, HMS Unbeaten surfaced and commenced patrolling close eastward of the supposed position of Kerkennah No.4 Buoy. (33)
5 Feb 1942 At 2027 B/5, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) surfaced and proceeded to patrol in position 122° - Kerkennah No.5 buoy - 38 miles as had been suggested by Capt. S.10. (33)

6 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 2309 B/6, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was informed by the Capt. S.10 that she had been reported by an enemy aircraft at 1104 B/6 and that the south-west corner of the patrol area had thus been compromised. Indeed, earlier that day, an aircraft had been seen circling overhead and further aircraft had been seen patrolling the area in the afternoon and at 2114 B/6, a large white aircraft flare had been dropped close astern after which HMS Unbeaten dived.
Upon receiving the above signal from the Capt. S.10 at 2330 B/6, course was set to proceed to the middle of the southern end of the patrol area. (33)

7 Feb 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) made a torpedo attack on the Italian transport Bosforo (3648 GRT, built 1929), which was on passage from Tripoli (sailed at 1745 B/6) to Palermo, escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Calliope. No hits were obtained though.

At 1005 B/7, Calliope sighted three torpedo tracks, two aimed at the transport and one aimed at her. She dropped depth charges set at 75 metres and abandoned the hunt at 1048 B/7 to rejoin Bosforo and caught up with her at 1145 B/7. An escorting aircraft also dropped a bomb.

At 1900 B/7, Calliope received a signal indicating that two British destroyers were sighted in position 35°35'N, 13°45'E, steering 300° at 26 knots. At 1958 B/7, a signal from Supermarina was received, ordering them to seek refuge in Sousse but Calliope replied that they were two hours ahead of schedule, they would instead proceed to Pantelleria where they arrived at 2315 B/7. At 0745 B/8, they sailed from Pantelleria with a Junker 88 providing air escort.

The two British destroyer reported in the signal received by Calliope at 1900 B/7 were HMS Zulu (Cdr. H.R. Graham, DSO, DSC, RN) and HMS Lively (Lt.Cdr. W.F.E. Hussey, DSC, RN) which had departed Malta around 1530 B/7 to intercept the Bosforo convoy and to patrol on the line Lampedusa / Pantelleria. They were to return to Malta at 0730 B/7. They did not find the Bosforo convoy but sank a trawler (this was the Italian naval auxiliary F 37 / Grongo (316 GRT, built 1924) and a schooner (this was the Italian naval auxiliary schooner V 87 / Aosta (494 GRT, built 1913). These vessels had been on passage from Lampedusa to Pantelleria. They were intercepted around 0130 B/8 in approximate position 35°56'N, 11°59'E. Both were sunk by gunfire from the British destroyers.

At 1740 B/8, Bosforo signalled that she had been attacked by a submarine (this was HMS Upholder (with Lt. C.P. Norman, RN in temporary command). Calliope went on to the attack, dropped a few depth charges, but found nothing. They arrived at Palermo at 2110 B/8.
0920 B/7 - Off Kerkennah, in position 34°25'N, 11°52'E, sighted a merchant vessel of about 5000 tons escorted by a Partenope-class torpedo boat. Started attack.
1004 B/7 - Fired four torpedoes from 3500 yards. No hits were obtained.
1014 B/7 - The escort vessel started a counter attack that lasted 20 minutes. 30 Depth charges were dropped all of which were rather close but no damage was sustained.
1116 B/7 - Attempted to have a good look round but was immediately put deep by an enemy aircraft. Course was set to proceed to the south-east corner of the patrol area. (35)

8 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1112 B/8, the Vice-Admiral Malta informed ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) and HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) of the following; One small merchant ship had been sighted at 0940 B/8 in position 35°50'N, 11°50'E, course 175° at 10 knots. (36)

11 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1415 B/11, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN); Leave patrol to arrive Marsamxett at 0720 B/14 by proceeding through the following positions; HMS Unbeaten 35°00'N, 12°25'E and 35°36'N, 14°10'E. HMS P 31 33°45'N, 12°35'E and 35°32'N, 14°14'E. Submarines encountered after 2200 B/13 are not to be attacked. (36)

12 Feb 1942 After having patrolled submerged near Kerkennah No.4 buoy during the day, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) surfaced at 1959 B/12 and set course to Malta in accordance with the orders in Capt. S.10's signal timed 0720 B/11.

At 2204 B/12, in approximate position 180° - Kerkennah No.5 buoy - 10 miles, while steering 065°, the glare of a flashing light seen over the horizon almost ahead. Course was altered towards to investigate.

In the next 9 minutes course was twice altered to keep the light ahead and the final course was 110°. The light was very irregular and showing a very short flash as from a torch about once or twice a minute.

At 2213 B/12, two lights were seen to flash on the starboard beam about a mile away, and the other one about one and a half mile away. It was thought the lights were carried by two enemy motor torpedo boats which were sweeping the area in line abreast flashing from time to time to maintain station. The night was rather dark. HMS Unbeaten dived but no HE was picked up.
At 2315 B/12, HMS Unbeaten surfaced and resumed her passage back to Malta.
At 2358 B/12, the same lights were seen far on the port beam showing they must have been travelling at a speed of over 12 knots to reach this position. (33)
14 Feb 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) ended her 17th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (33)

21 Feb 1942 At 1945 B/21, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) departed Malta for her 18th war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol the eastern approaches to Tripoli in position 32°44'N, 14°47'E. She was to proceeded though position 32°48'N, 14°45'E en-route.
Lt. Martin was still in command of Unbeaten for this patrol as Lt.Cdr. Woodward had strained his back during his leave and had been admitted to hospital.

This was to intercept the enemy convoy operation K 7. On 13 February, ULTRA had revealed the imminent departure of this convoy [see DEFE3/750 frames 77, 78, 153]. The transport Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Lince was to leave from Brindisi. The tanker Giulio Giordani (Italian, 10534 GRT, built 1939) and the transport Lerici (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Pallade were to leave from Taranto. They were to be screened by a task group formed around the battleship Caio Duilio. This operation was postponed, perhaps due to the presence of a Malta convoy (convoy MW 9A/B).

Eventually, the convoys were reorganised with three more ships from Messina and sailed on 20-21 February: 1) At 1935 B/20-2105 B/20, Giulio Giordani and Lerici sailed from Taranto, escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta (leader), Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Circe. 2) At 2205 B/20-2237 B/20, Monviso sailed from Brindisi escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini. 3) At 0345 B/21, the transport Unione (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1942) sailed from Naples at 0345 B/21 escorted by the torpedo boats Orsa and Enrico Cosenz and transited the Straits of Messina at 1730 B/21. 4) At 1730 B/21-1915 B/21, the transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Monginevro (Italian, 5324 GRT, built 1940) sailed from Messina, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale, Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Giuseppe Dezza and Pallade and merged with the Unione convoy.

To cover the operation the following forces were deployed: 1) At 1445 B21-1514 B/21, the battleship Caio Duilio, screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere sailed from Taranto. 2) At 1705 B/21-1845 B/21, the heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trento and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani and Antonio da Noli sailed from Messina. During the night, they caught up with the Ravello/Monginevro/Unione convoys.

At 0726 B/21-0754 B/21, the Giordani and Monviso convoys merged and proceeded to Corfu where they arrived at 1100 B/21-1126 B/21. At 1130 B/21-1400 B/21, they sailed from Corfu (without the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini, which later sailed for Valona).

At 0710 B/22, the Caio Duilio squadron sighted the Ravello/Monginevro/Unione convoy covered by the Gorizia squadron. Scirocco left the convoy as it crossed the 34th parallel and arrived at Messina at 1424 B/23. The Caio Duilio squadron turned back and arrived at Taranto at 0202 B/24-0230 B/24. The Gorizia squadron also left the convoy and returned to Messina, arriving at 1159 B/24-1340 B/24. The torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy and joined it at 1150 B/23. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1525 B/23-1735 B/23. (33) 22 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1018 B/22, the Capt. S.10 informed all submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla which were at sea that at 0925 B/22 three merchant ships, two cruisers and ten destroyers had been sighted in position 35°18'N, 18°40'E proceeding on course 190° at speeds of 8 to 12 knots In a signal timed 1111 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that eight merchant merchant ships, two battleships, five cruisers and fifteen destroyers had been sighted in position 35°12'N, 18°22'E on course 190° at a speed of 8 knots. In a signal timed 1336 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that an enemy battleship of the Littorio-class, two cruisers of the Trento / Condottieri-class, four destroyers, three merchant ships and three enemy aircraft had been reported in position 34°48'N, 18°30'E proceeding on course 190° at a speed of 10 knots. The following submarines were the ones that were on patrol at that time and thus must have received these signals; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN). Presumably HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) also received these signals as she was also on patrol but she was to the north of Sicily at that time so given the position of the convoy sighted the signals were of no use to her. (36) 23 Feb 1942 At 0700 B/23, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) dived while still 32 miles to the north of the ordered patrol position. She continued to proceed towards the ordered position at 3.3 knots submerged. At 0810 B/23, faint HE was heard on bearing 180° drawing westwards. Nothing was in sight. At 1000 B/23, the first of 118 underwater explosions was heard which lasted until 1250 A/23. At 1523 B/23, The first of 7 distant depth charges was heard to explode. This depth charging lasted for 3 minutes. At 1600 B/23, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1152 B/23 was received ordering HMS Unbeaten to patrol between Kuriat and Lampione Island. At 2000 B/23, HMS Unbeaten set course to proceed to her new patrol area as ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1152 B/23. (33) 23 Feb 1942 In signals timed 1152 B/23 and 1203 B/23, Capt. S.10 sent the following orders; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) leave their patrol positions at 2000 B/23 through positions 35°00'N, 14°12'E, and 35°00'N, 14°18'E respectively to arrive off Marsamxett harbour at 0715 B/25. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'K', 34°20'N, 11°10'E - 000° - 40 - 60 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E. HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'G', 35°05'N, 12°05'E - 000° - 50 - 40 through positions 33°05'N, 14°11'E and 35°00'N, 13°05'E. HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'L', south of 34°15'N and west of 11°40'E. She was to proceed through positions 33°00'N, 14°11'E and 33°50'N, 13°20'E. HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'H', 35°15'N, 11°00'E - 000° - 40 - 50 proceeding through positions 33°00'N, 14°32'E and 35°00'N, 12°30'E. HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'N', south of 33°45'N and between 11° 50'E and 12°55'E proceeding through positions 32°50'N, 14°45'E and 33.20'N, 13.45'E. HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'J', 36°54'N, 15°24'E - 330° - 40 - 70 proceeding through positions 32°38'N, 15°00'E and 35°00'N, 15°30'E. They were not to attack submarines encountered during the night of 23/24 February to prevent friendly fire incidents from happening. (36) 25 Feb 1942 At 2100 B/25, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) entered area 'H' in position 35°30'N, 12°00'E. (33) 28 Feb 1942 At 2000 B/27, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) surfaced and proceeded to a position 4 miles to the east of Sousse. During the 28th a periscope reconnaissances of Sousse harbour and Monastir Bay were carried out. At Sousse a 2500 tons transport painted in peace time colours was seen alongside and a 4000 tons transport painted grey was seen loading from an elevator. Nothing was seen in Monastir Bay. A Vichy-French motor launch type vessel was patrolling between Monastir and Sousse. At 2015 B/28, HMS Unbeaten surfaced and proceeded to position 160° - Kuriat lighthouse - 11 miles and she patrolled near that position during the night of 28 February / 1 March. (33) 1 Mar 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) torpedoed and sank the Vichy-French transport P.L.M. 20 (5417 GRT, built 1920) about 5 nautical miles east of Mehedia, Tunisia in position 35°26'N, 11°09'E. This was French convoy S 14 consisting of the above mentioned P.L.M. 20 as well as the transports Château Latour (1912 GRT, built 1916), Sainte Odette (4140 GRT, built 1938) and Sainte Simone (5535 GRT, built 1921) escorted by the torpedo boat Bombarde and the minesweeping sloop La Batailleuse. At the time of the attack, La Batailleuse and Sainte Simone were trailing behind as the latter could only make 5 knots. Bombarde dropped five depth charges but without causing damage to the attacker. La Batailleuse arrived on the scene and claimed to have been missed by a torpedo but this is not substantiated by HMS Unbeaten’s patrol report. P.L.M. 20 sank in less than two minutes, nine were killed or missing, thirty-two survivors were picked up. 0340 B/1 - Sighted a large French trawler trawling south of Kuriat with her lights on. Patrolled in position 090° - Mahedia lighthouse - 5 miles during the day. Investigated the wreck of a modern Italian transport of the Andrea Gritti-class. [No time given in the patrol report] Sighted a convoy of one tanker (P.L.M. 20 looked like a tanker, engine and funnel aft and the bridge amidships) of about 7000 tons and two merchant vessels of 6000 and 5000 tons. They were escorted by at least one but probably more destroyers. Started attack. 1938 B/1 - Fired four torpedoes at the tanker from 4000 yards. One hit was obtained. 1951 B/1 - A counter attack followed by what appeared to be two destroyers. Seven depth charges were dropped fairly close. Later eight more depth charges were dropped but these were not close. 2025 B/1 - Returned to periscope depth. Observed three or four vessels with steaming lights on, one facing south, the others north. A destroyer was signalling to the Citadel on Cape Mahedia which was answering. 2032 B/1 - Eight more depth charges were dropped. By now the battery was very low so Lt. Martin decided to retire to seaward to charge and return in the morning to investigate the area where the attack had taken place. At 2336 B/1, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1203 B/1 was received giving instructions on leaving patrol. (33) 1 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1203 B/1, the Capt. S.10 sent the following instructions on leaving patrol to the submarines mentioned; HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to arrive at Malta on 4 March and was to proceed through positions 35°40'N, 12°00'E and 35°40'N, 14°20'E on the way back. HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to arrive at Malta on 5 March and was to proceed through positions 34°00'N, 12°50'E and 35°32'N, 14°14'E on the way back. HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to arrive at Malta on 5 March and was to proceed through positions 37°02'N, 15°40'E and 35°51'N, 15°15'E on the way back. (37) 2 Mar 1942 During the forenoon, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) returned to the position of yesterday's attack. There was no sign of the vessel attacked but a certain amount of wood, oil, oranges and dead fish were seen in the water. At 2030 B/2, HMS Unbeaten surfaced and set course for Malta. At 2320 B/2, she dived on sighting a dark object which eventually turned out to be a crashed aircraft. At 0001 B/3, HMS Unbeaten surfaced and inspected the wreckage which turned out to be an Italian fighter aircraft. At 0015 B/3, HMS Unbeaten proceeded and continued her passage to Malta. (33) 3 Mar 1942 At 0420 B/3, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) dived on sighting a dark object on the horizon which was closed submerged to carry out an attack. In brilliant moonlight the vessel was clearly illuminated and passed on a 90° track 1200 yards from the submarine. The vessel was thought to be the Italian Umberto Lupi a large armed trawler. This vessel was now named Amba Aradam (Italian, 405 GRT, built 1936) and was in use as a refrigeration ship. Enemy course was 191° at 12 knots. On this course she would have passed close west of Lampedusa. It was considered that this vessel was on passage and not patrolling. It could however not have been the Amba Aradam as this ship was at Benghazi at this time having arrived from Tripoli the previous day. Most likely the vessel encountered was the naval auxiliary D 22 / Lago Tana (783 GRT, built 1940) which had sailed from Trapani at 1530 B/2 and arrived at Lampedusa at 0630 B/3. At 0520 B/3, HMS Unbeaten surfaced and continued passage to Malta. At 2110 B/3, in position 225° - Cape San Dimitri [Gozo Island, Malta] - 45 miles, HE was heard on bearing 300° which was thought to be originating from a submarine for which HMS Unbeaten started a search until the HE faded around 2205 B/3 after which HMS Unbeaten proceeded. (33) 4 Mar 1942 At 0055 B/4, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted a dark object on the horizon ahead and heard it's HE. The object was seen to dive and HMS Unbeaten immediately did the same. SS/T communication was then established with ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski) which had also seen the other submarine at roughly the same time. (38) 4 Mar 1942 At 0700 B/4, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) ended her 18th war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (33) 12 Mar 1942 At 2030 B/12, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Malta for her 19th war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in area 'N' to the south of the Straits of Messina. Area 'N' was enclosed by the following positions; 37°13'N, 16°02'E, 37°31'N, 16°40'E, 38°05'N, 16°15'E, 37°48'N, 15°37'E. From dawn on the 17th she was to patrol in position 37°47'N, 15°45'E. At 2050 B/12, in position 080° - St. Elmo - 2 miles loud HE was heard to the northward. This is thought to have been an enemy motor torpedo boat. Nothing could be seen though but at 2058 B/12, HMS Unbeaten dived. At 2123 B/12, an enemy report was passed. (33) 13 Mar 1942 At 2320 B/13, in position 36°57'N, 15°53'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) picked up the HE of three destroyers on bearing 170°. Course was set to the eastward to try to intercept but the vessels passed to the southward without being sighted. The night was very dark. At 2346 B/13, funnel smoke was smelt. [No Italian destroyers or torpedo boats are know to have have been in this area at that time.] (33) 15 Mar 1942 At 0930 B/15, while patrolling near Cape Spartivento, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted two motor torpedo boats proceeding westwards at speed very close inshore. At 0945 B/15, two small minesweepers swept the channel from west to east 2 miles from the coast. At 1000 B/15, a motor torpedo boat was seen laying stopped in position 160° - Cape dell'Armi - 5 miles. This was possibly either MAS 536 or MAS 562 which both sailed for a patrol (from Messina?) and returned to Messina at 1025 B/15. At 1020 B/15, the motor torpedo boat got underway and proceeded to the north-westward. At 1045 B/15, a destroyer was seen proceeding westwards along the swept channel. This was possibly the Italian torpedo boat Francesco Stocco which had sailed from Messina at 1720 B/14 for an A/S sweep and returned at 1310 B/15. At 1115 B/15, the two small minesweepers returned along the swept channel from the eastward. (33) 16 Mar 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) fires four torpedoes at the Italian transport Vettor Pisani (6339 GRT, built 1939) off Cape Buzzano, Calabria, Italy. All torpedoes however missed their target. The target, which was later to join up with other ships, was on passage from Messina to Tripoli escorted by the italian destroyers Lanzerotto Malocello, Ugolino Vivaldi and the Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori. The escorting CANT Z.501 (4/184^ Squadriglia) sighted a torpedo fired from about 2000 meters and reported it and dropped two 160 kg bombs on the presumed point of origin but could not ascertain the result. At the same time Sirtori fired a round to give the alarm and rushed to the attack dropping four depth charges while Malocello turned back and dropped 16 depth charges. An axis supply convoy was formed in three sections (operation SIRIO): The Vettor Pisani section sailed from Messina at 1330 B/16-1350 B/16. Giuseppe Sirtori turned back during the evening and arrived at Messina at 0220 B/17. The remaining ships proceeded to Tripoli. At 0715 B/17, the Vettor Pisani section united with another section made up of the German transport Reichenfels (7744 GRT, built 1936) escorted by the Italian destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the Italian torpedo boat Pallade which had sailed from Messina at 0720 B/16. A covering force was provided by the Italian light cruiser Emanuele Filiberto Duca d’Aosta (7th Cruiser Division) and the Italian destroyers Nicolò Zeno, Grecale and Scirocco which sailed from Taranto at 1450 B/16-1800 B/16. At 1230 B/17, the convoy sighted the covering force which escorted them until 2000 B/17, in 33°47’20”N, 17°48’50”E, when it turned back to Italy, except Nicolò Zeno which remained with the convoy. At the same time, the convoy split in two: 1) Reichenfels escorted by Emanuele Pessagno and Lanzerotto Malocello. 2) Vettor Pisani escorted by Ugolino Vivaldi, Nicolò Zeno and Pallade. At 0800 B/18, the two convoys reunited. The escort was reinforced by the torpedo boat Generale Marcello Prestinari which had sailed from Tripoli at 2200 B/17. They arrived at Tripoli at 1430 B/18-1600 B/18. Emanuele Filiberto Duca d’Aosta and the destroyers Grecale and Scirocco arrived at Taranto at 1825-2225 B/18. The third section was made of the Italian transport Assunta di Gregori (4219 GRT, built 1914) escorted by the destroyer Premuda which sailed from Palermo at 0158 B/16-0230 B/16 and was reinforced by the torpedo boat Castore which sailed from Trapani at 0800 B/16. They never united with the other two sections but followed the Tunisian route instead and arrived at Tripoli at 0712 B/19-0736 B/19. 1137 B/16 - In position 38°01'N, 16°15'E, smoke was sighted bearing 200°. Visibility was very poor, misty and patchy. 1145 B/16 - A destroyer was sighted bearing 260°. Unbeaten closed at full speed. 1150 B/16 - The faint outline of a merchant ship was visible on bearing 290° at a range of about 5 miles. Unbeaten continued to close at full speed but an attack in such poor visibility was impossible from long range. Unbeaten was already abaft the targets beam. Three aircraft was patrolling nearby, obviously air escorts of this merchant vessel. --------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 1600 B/16 - In position 38°00'N, 16°11'E sighted a CANT flying boat to the southward. 1637 B/16 - Sighted a merchant vessel escorted by a destroyer bearing 241°. Range was about 7 nautical miles. They were proceeding close inshore. Started attack. 1645 B/16 - The merchant vessel was a large one of about 11000 tons. She was now seen to be escorted by three destroyers. 1706 B/16 - Fired four torpedoes from 4000 yards. 1710 B/16 - Almost four minutes after firing 2 loud explosions were heard. HE from the target, which had been strong, stopped. Two of the destroyers made a counter attack. 1800 B/16 - Returned to periscope depth and sighted a Generali-class torpedo boat only 500 yards off. Went deep again. 1825 B/16 - Last depth charges dropped. 1835 B/16 - Two destroyers were seen stopped in the place of the attack. There was no sign of the target. 1850 B/16 - Cleared the area to the south-east. (33) 17 Mar 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian submarine Guglielmotti off Cape Dell'Armi, Italy in position 37°42'N, 15°58'E. According to Italian sources she had been on passage from Taranto to Messina. The torpedo boat Francesco Stocco arrived on the scene, dropped 17 depth charges and picked up one body but there were no survivors. 0633 B/17 - In position 37°42'N, 15°58'E heard HE bearing 130°. 0635 B/17 - Sighted a submarine bearing 125°, distant 2200 yards, manoeuvred into attack position. 0640 B/17 - Fired four torpedoes. One minute and 40 seconds after firing an explosion was heard, HE stopped and the submarine was heard breaking up. 0720 B/17 - Surfaced to pick up survivors. There were about 12 in the water but Unbeaten was forced to dive by an approaching aircraft and clear the area. 1005 B/17 - Aircraft and motor torpedo boats were seen in the area of the sinking. 1010 B/17 to 1020 B/17 - Distant depth charging was heard. Twenty-four depth charges were dropped by the three motor torpedo boats present. Unbeaten was now out of torpedoes so course was set to Malta to take on board new torpedoes. (33) 19 Mar 1942 Around 0830 B/19, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) arrived at Malta to take on board new torpedoes. Around 2000 B/19 she departed again to resume her patrol. She was to patrol in position 37°47'N, 15°45'E. En-route to that position she was to pass through position 37°10'N, 16°02'E. (33) 21 Mar 1942 At 0632 B/21, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) was in position 37°37'N, 15°56'E when HE was heard bearing 200° which was closed. It was still very dark. At 0640 B/21, smoke was sighted bearing 200°. It was closed at speed. At 0650 B/21, a 3500 tons transport and two destroyers were sighted bearing 195°. Enemy course was about 100°. Range was 17000 yards. The attack was broken off as the enemy was well out of range. This was most probably the German transport Brook (1225 GRT, built 1927) escorted by the Italian destroyer Strale and the Italian torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari which sailed from Messina at 0015 B/21-0100 B/21. They were attacked by torpedo bombers at 0415 B/21, 18 miles south of Cape dell’Armi. They arrived at Argostoli at 0845 B/22. At 1600 B/21, a Generali-class torpedo boat was sighted proceeding westwards close inshore along the Calabrian coast. [We have been unable to identify this vessels, no destroyers or torpedo boats should have been in the area at this time.] At 2240 B/21, HE of a single destroyer was picked up which appeared to be patrolling in the vicinity of position 37°52'N, 15°57'E some 12 miles, 110° from Cape dell'Armi. This destroyer remained on patrol until 0100 B/22. Unbeaten retired to the southward to charge.
