This was most probably HE from the Italian torpedo boat Francesco Stocco which sailed from Messina at 2045 B/21 for an A/S sweep and returned at 1045 B/22. (33) 24 Mar 1942 At 1706 B/24, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1426 B/24, which ordered her to leave patrol leave patrol so as to arrive off Malta A.M. on the 26th. She was to proceed through position 37°15'N, 16°02'E on the way back to Malta. HMS Unbeaten immediately altered course for Malta to comply with these orders. (39) 25 Mar 1942 At 0140 B/25, (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0040 B/25, 0109 B/25 and 0121 B/25 which stated the following; 0040 B/25; Two enemy cruisers at 2350 B/24 in position 36°25'N, 16°48'E, course 330° speed 15 knots. (this position was amended later in the signal timed 0121 B/25, see below). Albacores will attack around 0300 B/25. 0109 B/25; My 0040 B/25, endeavour to intercept. 0121 B/25; My 0040 B/25, amend position to 36°45'N, 16°45'E. HMS Unbeaten immediately altered course to 020° and increased speed to full. Weather was bad so speed made good was only 7 knots. At 0310 B/25, in position 37°03'N, 15°54'E, sighted aircraft flares bearing 110° well over the horizon. Course was altered to 055° to try to intercept. The bearing of the flares, which were dropped intermittently maintained a steady bearing of 110° but remained well over the horizon. At 0415 B/25, the last flare was sighted. At 0545 B/25, the chase was abandoned and dived as dawn was approaching. Nothing was in sight and no HE was picked up after diving. No enemy cruisers had been in this area. These were the Italian destroyer Folgore and the Italian torpedo boat Pallade searching for survivors of the destroyers Lanciere and Scirocco which had foundered in bad weather. At 2340 B/25, HMS Unbeaten passed her signal timed 2142 B/25 to Capt. S.10. This signal contained her time of arrival at Malta which was given as 0900 B/27. A weather report was also included. (39) 26 Mar 1942 Around 0900 B/26, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) ended her 19th war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (33) 1 Apr 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) was damaged by enemy bombing while laying submerged at Malta. The damage could not be repaired at Malta due to the heavy air raids and it was decided that Unbeaten had to go to Gibraltar for docking and repairs. Damage to HMS Unbeaten consisted of one cracked container, number thre bow cap leaking badly, number one slightly. Dent in pressure hull plate and leaking rivet in tube space and stowage compartment. Small leak in number one battery sump which necessitates docking for about 2 days. It was intended to sail her to Gibraltar, from where after a docking and rest period, the Capt. S.8 was to sail her on patrol in the Gulf of Genoa and thence via west Coast Sardinia to Malta. (5) 9 Apr 1942 At 0530 B/9, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) departed Malta for Gibraltar. En-route she was to make rendezvous with HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) to take on board Capt. Wilson, RM and transport him to Gibraltar. [No log is available for this period so no map of the passage to the U.K. can be displayed.] (5) 11 Apr 1942 At 0340 B/11, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) made rendezvous with HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) in position 35°34'N, 12°17'E. She then took on board Capt. Wilson, RM and proceeded towards Gibraltar. The crew of HMS Unbeaten were the last to see HMS Upholder which did not return from her patrol. (5) 12 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1508 B/12, F.O.C.N.A. (Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic), informed HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) of her onward route to Gibraltar; From 38°20'N, 09°00'E at 1200 B/14 to 38°12'N, 01°01'E at 0600 B/18 then to 36°31'N, 02°12'W at 0330 B/20 to arrive Gibraltar Gate at 1200 B/21. Do not pass 05°00'W before 0830 B/21. Report if unable to comply within 3 hours. Bombing restrictions are arranged. At 1745 B/12, in position 37°24'N, 12°29'E, the mast of a northbound merchant vessel were seen. Two fighters appeared to be overhead as air escorts. This was most probably the Italian transport Gino Allegri (6836 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Centauro which sailed from Tripoli at 1900 B/11. At 1412 B/12, they were near Pantelleria where they temporary picked up an additional escort of the motor torpedo boats MAS 557 and MAS 564 which had sailed from Pantelleria at 1330 B/12 and returned at 1640 B/12. Ginp Allegri and Centauro arrived at Naples at 1057 B/13. (40) 16 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1308 B/16, F.O.C.N.A. (Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic) sent the following orders to HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN): From 38°12'N, 01°01'E amend route to pass through 36°35'N, 02°12'W at 0330 B/20 and then through 36°27'N, 04°27'E at 0430 B/21 thence on the surface to arrive off Gibraltar gate at 1200 B/21. (41) 19 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 2111 B/19, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) sent the following signal to F.O.C.N.A. (Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic); E.T.A. 36°27'N, 04°27'N at 0900 B/21. Propose diving at 0001 B/22 in 36°14'N, 05°00' surfacing 180° - Europa Point - 1 mile at 0930 B/22. Request approval own arrangements. F.O.C.N.A. replied in a signal timed 2340 B/19 as follows; Your 2111 B/19 not approved for operational reasons. Proceed on surface from 36 27'N, 04°27'W direct to arrive Gibraltar 1600 B/21. (41) 21 Apr 1942 Around 1830 B/21, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) arrived at Gibraltar for repairs before being able to be deployed operationally. She was taken in hand for repairs on April 29th. On docking it however became apparent that it was not considered wise to sent HMS Unbeaten on patrol until permanent damage repairs had been made. It was decided that she was to proceed to the U.K. to be taken in hand for refit at the Chatham Dockyard which now had capacity available as HMS Upholder which was supposed to have refitted there had not returned from patrol earlier in April. (5) 26 May 1942 Around 1500 B/26, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) departed Gibraltar for the U.K. She was to cross 43°N in 12°10'W at 0800 B/1. From there she was to proceed dived by day through positions 46°01'N, 12°10'W, 49°05'N, 12°06'N. 49°30'N, 08°01'W to R/V as ordered by the Capt. S.5 in the vicinity of Lizard Head on 8 June. In a signal timed 1551 B/2, the Capt. S.5 ordered her to make rendezvous in position 180° - Lizard Head - 5 miles at 0700 B/8. In a signal timed 1221 B/4, the Capt. S.5 amended the rendezvous position to 180° - Lizard Head - 10 miles. In a signal timed 0015 B/6, HMS Unbeaten informed the Capt. S.5 that she was not able to make the rendezvous at the time ordered and she requested instructions. In a signal timed 1221 B/4, the Capt. S.5 sent her the following orders; Amend route to pass through positions 180° - Bishop Rock - 15 miles and 180° - Lizard Head - 15 miles, thence to the rendezvous at 1030 B/7. (10) 7 Jun 1942 Around 1000 B/7 - 1130 B/7, HMS Torbay (Cdr. A.C.C. Miers, DSO and Bar, RN) and HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) made rendezvous in position 180° - Lizard Head - 10 miles, with their escort towards Portsmouth HMS Kingston Jacinth (Skr. A.G. Day, RNR). (42) 8 Jun 1942 Around 0930 B/8, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) arrived at Portsmouth (Gosport / Fort Blockhouse) from Gibraltar. (10) 12 Jun 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) departed Portsmouth for Sheerness. She took passage in convoy CE 89 (St. Helens Roads - Southend) which was escorted by HMS Garth (Lt.Cdr. J.P. Scatchard, RN). (5) 13 Jun 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) arrived at Sheerness. She proceeded to Chatham Dockyard later the same day escorted by HM ML 113 (?). (43) 14 Jun 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) started her refit at Chatham Dockyard. (5) 15 Sep 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) ended her refit at the Chatham Dockyard. [As no logs are available for HMS Unbeaten from February 1942 until her loss, details will be missing.] (5) 17 Sep 1942 With her refit at the Chatham dockyard completed HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) departed Sheerness around 1345 A/17 for Portsmouth. She made the passage together with HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) which had just completed damage repairs at the Chatham Dockyard. They were escorted by the barrage balloon vessel HMS Bungay (T/Lt. G.D. Davies, RNR). (44) 18 Sep 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) and HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) arrive at Portsmouth (Gosport, HMS Dolphin) from Sheerness. (45) 21 Sep 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) departed Portsmouth for Plymouth. She was escorted by HMS Lord Stonehaven (Skr. A.F.S. Jenner, RNR). (45) 22 Sep 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) arrived at Plymouth. At Plymouth (Devonport Dockyard) a radar set was fitted and R.D.F. trials followed on completion. (45) 4 Oct 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) departed Plymouth for Holy Loch. She was escorted to the vicinity of Trevose Head by HMS Cambridgeshire (T/Lt. H.F.B. Ashby, RNVR) where HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR) took over. (5) 6 Oct 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) arrived at Holy Loch to begin a period of training. [As no log is available no details can be given.] (45) 23 Oct 1942 Around 1700 A/23, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) departed Holy Loch for her 20th war patrol. This was the 1st patrol of her 2nd commission. She was ordered to patrol in the Bay of Biscay (off Northern Spain) and to perform a special operation (Operation Bluestone), landing a special agent near Baiona, Spain (near Vigo). Passage south through the Irish Sea was made together with HMS P 614 (Lt. H.W. Wilkinson, RN). The submarines were escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN). The submarines parted company with the escort around 2110 A/25 when west of Bishop Rock. From there HMS Unbeaten was to proceed to her patrol area through positions; (E) 50°01'N, 08°01'W. (F) 50°01'N, 13°01'W. (G) 45°01'N, 13°01'W. (H) 43°30'N, 12°01'W, E.T.A. 0600 A/30. (46) 2 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1930 A/2, to the Flag Officer Submarines HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) informed him that the special operation had been carried out at 2359 A/1. Un the signal it was also stated that at 1000 A/2 a ship similar in appearance to the Portuguese Angola (7745 GRT, built 1912) wearing Spanish markings had been seen, west of Vigo, in position 42°19'N, 10°00'W proceeding on course 020° at 10 knots. HMS Unbeaten had been unable to identify this ship positively. In a signal timed 2212 A/2, F.O.S. replied as follows; Your 1930 A/2. Proceed forthwith diving by day to area K 501 for operations in accordance with your patrol orders. (47) 6 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1141 A/6, the Flag Officer Submarines sent the following orders to HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN); Reported at 1100 A/6 a 6000-ton merchant ship in position 44°58'N, 05°10'W proceeding on course 270°, speed 12 knots. Proceed to intercept, bombing restrictions extended. In a signal timed 1334 A/6, F.O.S. informed HMS Unbeaten that aircraft report the enemy merchant ship at 1221 A/6 now in position 44°46'N, 05°43'W, course 270°, speed 10 knots. In a signal timed 1522 A/6, F.O.S. informed HMS Unbeaten that the merchant vessel's position at 1340 A/6 was 44°56'N, 05°58'W and that the enemy's speed may be reduced. Three aircraft strikes have been arranged in Bay of Biscay area. At 1920 A/6, a signal timed 1810 A/6, was received which reported that an enemy supply vessel had been sighted in position 45°00'N, 07°00'W on bearing 055° and at a range of 7 miles. Enemy course was 135°, speed 11 knots. The signal was a bit garbled but it is presumed this signal came from HMS Unbeaten as no other HM ship was to be in that position at that time. In a signal timed 2109 A/6, F.O.S. sent the following orders to HMS Unbeaten; If no contact has been made with enemy by dawn 7th November proceed to patrol vicinity of position 45°01'N, 07°59'W. These signals referred to the German blockade breaker Anneliese Essberger (5173 GRT, built 1935) which had left the Gironde estuary in the evening of 5 November 1942 for the Far East. (48) 7 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1901 A/7, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) received the following signal from the Capt. S.3; leave patrol area after dark 8th, dive by day and pass through the following positions at times stated; (A) 44°31'N, 06°59'W at 0030 A/9. (B) 45°05'N, 04°02'W at 0030 A/10. (C) 47°55'N, 07°59'W at 2000 A/11. (D) 180° - Bishops Rock - 10 miles at 1700 A/12. In position D rendezvous was to be made with HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN). (10) 11 Nov 1942 At 0216 A/11, a submarine was detected steering 330° to 340° in position 46°55'N, 06°51'W by Wellington 'F' of No.172 squadron RAF Coastal Command. This S/M was attacked at 0235 A/11. She was straddled with depth charges and attacked with machine guns. 30 minutes later the attacking aircraft reported that the submarine had turned 90° to starboard and was stationary. The attack position is 12 miles inside a submerged bombing restrictions area with total bombing restrictions at night. The attack position was only 10 miles from the estimated position by the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) of HMS Unbeaten. The attacking aircraft also noticed radar interference. HMS Unbeaten was equipped with radar. No German submarine was missing following this attack and it is very likely the submarine attacked and sunk was HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) which should have been in this area around this time. The aircraft was not aware of the bombing restrictions which were in place. In a signal timed 0514 A/11 the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) informed HMS Unbeaten of this attack. In a signal timed 0739 A/11, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) informed HMS Unbeaten that a Sunderland aircraft escorted by two Beaufighters were proceeding towards position 46°55'N, 06°51'W with object of escorting you should you be unable to dive. From the above signal it is apparent that it must have been realised that the submarine attacked by the aircraft may have been HMS Unbeaten. In a signal timed 1319 A/11, F.O.S. ordered HMS Unbeaten to report her position, course and speed. The signal was transmitted four timed but there came no reply. Aircraft searches on 11, 12, 13 and 14 November failed to find any sign of the submarine or it's crew. The torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy and joined it at 1150 B/23. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1525 B/23-1735 B/23. (33)

22 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1018 B/22, the Capt. S.10 informed all submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla which were at sea that at 0925 B/22 three merchant ships, two cruisers and ten destroyers had been sighted in position 35°18'N, 18°40'E proceeding on course 190° at speeds of 8 to 12 knots

In a signal timed 1111 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that eight merchant merchant ships, two battleships, five cruisers and fifteen destroyers had been sighted in position 35°12'N, 18°22'E on course 190° at a speed of 8 knots.

In a signal timed 1336 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that an enemy battleship of the Littorio-class, two cruisers of the Trento / Condottieri-class, four destroyers, three merchant ships and three enemy aircraft had been reported in position 34°48'N, 18°30'E proceeding on course 190° at a speed of 10 knots.

The following submarines were the ones that were on patrol at that time and thus must have received these signals; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN).

Presumably HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) also received these signals as she was also on patrol but she was to the north of Sicily at that time so given the position of the convoy sighted the signals were of no use to her. (36)

23 Feb 1942 At 0700 B/23, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) dived while still 32 miles to the north of the ordered patrol position. She continued to proceed towards the ordered position at 3.3 knots submerged.
At 0810 B/23, faint HE was heard on bearing 180° drawing westwards. Nothing was in sight.
At 1000 B/23, the first of 118 underwater explosions was heard which lasted until 1250 A/23.
At 1523 B/23, The first of 7 distant depth charges was heard to explode. This depth charging lasted for 3 minutes.
At 1600 B/23, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1152 B/23 was received ordering HMS Unbeaten to patrol between Kuriat and Lampione Island.
At 2000 B/23, HMS Unbeaten set course to proceed to her new patrol area as ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1152 B/23. (33)

23 Feb 1942 In signals timed 1152 B/23 and 1203 B/23, Capt. S.10 sent the following orders; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) leave their patrol positions at 2000 B/23 through positions 35°00'N, 14°12'E, and 35°00'N, 14°18'E respectively to arrive off Marsamxett harbour at 0715 B/25.
HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'K', 34°20'N, 11°10'E - 000° - 40 - 60 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E.
HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'G', 35°05'N, 12°05'E - 000° - 50 - 40 through positions 33°05'N, 14°11'E and 35°00'N, 13°05'E.

HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'L', south of 34°15'N and west of 11°40'E. She was to proceed through positions 33°00'N, 14°11'E and 33°50'N, 13°20'E.

HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'H', 35°15'N, 11°00'E - 000° - 40 - 50 proceeding through positions 33°00'N, 14°32'E and 35°00'N, 12°30'E.

HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'N', south of 33°45'N and between 11° 50'E and 12°55'E proceeding through positions 32°50'N, 14°45'E and 33.20'N, 13.45'E.

HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'J', 36°54'N, 15°24'E - 330° - 40 - 70 proceeding through positions 32°38'N, 15°00'E and 35°00'N, 15°30'E.
They were not to attack submarines encountered during the night of 23/24 February to prevent friendly fire incidents from happening. (36)
25 Feb 1942 At 2100 B/25, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) entered area 'H' in position 35°30'N, 12°00'E. (33)
28 Feb 1942 At 2000 B/27, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) surfaced and proceeded to a position 4 miles to the east of Sousse.

During the 28th a periscope reconnaissances of Sousse harbour and Monastir Bay were carried out. At Sousse a 2500 tons transport painted in peace time colours was seen alongside and a 4000 tons transport painted grey was seen loading from an elevator. Nothing was seen in Monastir Bay. A Vichy-French motor launch type vessel was patrolling between Monastir and Sousse.
At 2015 B/28, HMS Unbeaten surfaced and proceeded to position 160° - Kuriat lighthouse - 11 miles and she patrolled near that position during the night of 28 February / 1 March. (33)

1 Mar 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) torpedoed and sank the Vichy-French transport P.L.M. 20 (5417 GRT, built 1920) about 5 nautical miles east of Mehedia, Tunisia in position 35°26'N, 11°09'E.

This was French convoy S 14 consisting of the above mentioned P.L.M. 20 as well as the transports Château Latour (1912 GRT, built 1916), Sainte Odette (4140 GRT, built 1938) and Sainte Simone (5535 GRT, built 1921) escorted by the torpedo boat Bombarde and the minesweeping sloop La Batailleuse.

At the time of the attack, La Batailleuse and Sainte Simone were trailing behind as the latter could only make 5 knots. Bombarde dropped five depth charges but without causing damage to the attacker. La Batailleuse arrived on the scene and claimed to have been missed by a torpedo but this is not substantiated by HMS Unbeaten’s patrol report. P.L.M. 20 sank in less than two minutes, nine were killed or missing, thirty-two survivors were picked up.

0340 B/1 - Sighted a large French trawler trawling south of Kuriat with her lights on. Patrolled in position 090° - Mahedia lighthouse - 5 miles during the day. Investigated the wreck of a modern Italian transport of the Andrea Gritti-class.

[No time given in the patrol report] Sighted a convoy of one tanker (P.L.M. 20 looked like a tanker, engine and funnel aft and the bridge amidships) of about 7000 tons and two merchant vessels of 6000 and 5000 tons. They were escorted by at least one but probably more destroyers. Started attack.
1938 B/1 - Fired four torpedoes at the tanker from 4000 yards. One hit was obtained.
1951 B/1 - A counter attack followed by what appeared to be two destroyers. Seven depth charges were dropped fairly close. Later eight more depth charges were dropped but these were not close.

2025 B/1 - Returned to periscope depth. Observed three or four vessels with steaming lights on, one facing south, the others north. A destroyer was signalling to the Citadel on Cape Mahedia which was answering.

2032 B/1 - Eight more depth charges were dropped. By now the battery was very low so Lt. Martin decided to retire to seaward to charge and return in the morning to investigate the area where the attack had taken place. At 2336 B/1, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1203 B/1 was received giving instructions on leaving patrol. (33)

1 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1203 B/1, the Capt. S.10 sent the following instructions on leaving patrol to the submarines mentioned; HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to arrive at Malta on 4 March and was to proceed through positions 35°40'N, 12°00'E and 35°40'N, 14°20'E on the way back. HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to arrive at Malta on 5 March and was to proceed through positions 34°00'N, 12°50'E and 35°32'N, 14°14'E on the way back. HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to arrive at Malta on 5 March and was to proceed through positions 37°02'N, 15°40'E and 35°51'N, 15°15'E on the way back. (37)

2 Mar 1942 During the forenoon, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) returned to the position of yesterday's attack. There was no sign of the vessel attacked but a certain amount of wood, oil, oranges and dead fish were seen in the water.
At 2030 B/2, HMS Unbeaten surfaced and set course for Malta.
At 2320 B/2, she dived on sighting a dark object which eventually turned out to be a crashed aircraft.
At 0001 B/3, HMS Unbeaten surfaced and inspected the wreckage which turned out to be an Italian fighter aircraft.
At 0015 B/3, HMS Unbeaten proceeded and continued her passage to Malta. (33)

3 Mar 1942 At 0420 B/3, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) dived on sighting a dark object on the horizon which was closed submerged to carry out an attack. In brilliant moonlight the vessel was clearly illuminated and passed on a 90° track 1200 yards from the submarine. The vessel was thought to be the Italian Umberto Lupi a large armed trawler. This vessel was now named Amba Aradam (Italian, 405 GRT, built 1936) and was in use as a refrigeration ship. Enemy course was 191° at 12 knots. On this course she would have passed close west of Lampedusa. It was considered that this vessel was on passage and not patrolling.
It could however not have been the Amba Aradam as this ship was at Benghazi at this time having arrived from Tripoli the previous day.
Most likely the vessel encountered was the naval auxiliary D 22 / Lago Tana (783 GRT, built 1940) which had sailed from Trapani at 1530 B/2 and arrived at Lampedusa at 0630 B/3.
At 0520 B/3, HMS Unbeaten surfaced and continued passage to Malta.

At 2110 B/3, in position 225° - Cape San Dimitri [Gozo Island, Malta] - 45 miles, HE was heard on bearing 300° which was thought to be originating from a submarine for which HMS Unbeaten started a search until the HE faded around 2205 B/3 after which HMS Unbeaten proceeded. (33)

4 Mar 1942 At 0055 B/4, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted a dark object on the horizon ahead and heard it's HE. The object was seen to dive and HMS Unbeaten immediately did the same. SS/T communication was then established with ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski) which had also seen the other submarine at roughly the same time. (38)
4 Mar 1942 At 0700 B/4, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) ended her 18th war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (33)

12 Mar 1942 At 2030 B/12, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Malta for her 19th war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in area 'N' to the south of the Straits of Messina. Area 'N' was enclosed by the following positions; 37°13'N, 16°02'E, 37°31'N, 16°40'E, 38°05'N, 16°15'E, 37°48'N, 15°37'E.
From dawn on the 17th she was to patrol in position 37°47'N, 15°45'E.

At 2050 B/12, in position 080° - St. Elmo - 2 miles loud HE was heard to the northward. This is thought to have been an enemy motor torpedo boat. Nothing could be seen though but at 2058 B/12, HMS Unbeaten dived.
At 2123 B/12, an enemy report was passed. (33)

13 Mar 1942 At 2320 B/13, in position 36°57'N, 15°53'E, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) picked up the HE of three destroyers on bearing 170°. Course was set to the eastward to try to intercept but the vessels passed to the southward without being sighted. The night was very dark. At 2346 B/13, funnel smoke was smelt.
[No Italian destroyers or torpedo boats are know to have have been in this area at that time.] (33)
15 Mar 1942 At 0930 B/15, while patrolling near Cape Spartivento, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) sighted two motor torpedo boats proceeding westwards at speed very close inshore.
At 0945 B/15, two small minesweepers swept the channel from west to east 2 miles from the coast.
At 1000 B/15, a motor torpedo boat was seen laying stopped in position 160° - Cape dell'Armi - 5 miles.
This was possibly either MAS 536 or MAS 562 which both sailed for a patrol (from Messina?) and returned to Messina at 1025 B/15.
At 1020 B/15, the motor torpedo boat got underway and proceeded to the north-westward.
At 1045 B/15, a destroyer was seen proceeding westwards along the swept channel.
This was possibly the Italian torpedo boat Francesco Stocco which had sailed from Messina at 1720 B/14 for an A/S sweep and returned at 1310 B/15.
At 1115 B/15, the two small minesweepers returned along the swept channel from the eastward. (33)

16 Mar 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) fires four torpedoes at the Italian transport Vettor Pisani (6339 GRT, built 1939) off Cape Buzzano, Calabria, Italy. All torpedoes however missed their target.

The target, which was later to join up with other ships, was on passage from Messina to Tripoli escorted by the italian destroyers Lanzerotto Malocello, Ugolino Vivaldi and the Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori. The escorting CANT Z.501 (4/184^ Squadriglia) sighted a torpedo fired from about 2000 meters and reported it and dropped two 160 kg bombs on the presumed point of origin but could not ascertain the result. At the same time Sirtori fired a round to give the alarm and rushed to the attack dropping four depth charges while Malocello turned back and dropped 16 depth charges.

An axis supply convoy was formed in three sections (operation SIRIO): The Vettor Pisani section sailed from Messina at 1330 B/16-1350 B/16. Giuseppe Sirtori turned back during the evening and arrived at Messina at 0220 B/17. The remaining ships proceeded to Tripoli.

At 0715 B/17, the Vettor Pisani section united with another section made up of the German transport Reichenfels (7744 GRT, built 1936) escorted by the Italian destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the Italian torpedo boat Pallade which had sailed from Messina at 0720 B/16.

A covering force was provided by the Italian light cruiser Emanuele Filiberto Duca d’Aosta (7th Cruiser Division) and the Italian destroyers Nicolò Zeno, Grecale and Scirocco which sailed from Taranto at 1450 B/16-1800 B/16.
At 1230 B/17, the convoy sighted the covering force which escorted them until 2000 B/17, in 33°47’20”N, 17°48’50”E, when it turned back to Italy, except Nicolò Zeno which remained with the convoy.
At the same time, the convoy split in two: 1) Reichenfels escorted by Emanuele Pessagno and Lanzerotto Malocello. 2) Vettor Pisani escorted by Ugolino Vivaldi, Nicolò Zeno and Pallade.
At 0800 B/18, the two convoys reunited. The escort was reinforced by the torpedo boat Generale Marcello Prestinari which had sailed from Tripoli at 2200 B/17.
They arrived at Tripoli at 1430 B/18-1600 B/18.
Emanuele Filiberto Duca d’Aosta and the destroyers Grecale and Scirocco arrived at Taranto at 1825-2225 B/18.

The third section was made of the Italian transport Assunta di Gregori (4219 GRT, built 1914) escorted by the destroyer Premuda which sailed from Palermo at 0158 B/16-0230 B/16 and was reinforced by the torpedo boat Castore which sailed from Trapani at 0800 B/16. They never united with the other two sections but followed the Tunisian route instead and arrived at Tripoli at 0712 B/19-0736 B/19.
1137 B/16 - In position 38°01'N, 16°15'E, smoke was sighted bearing 200°. Visibility was very poor, misty and patchy.
1145 B/16 - A destroyer was sighted bearing 260°. Unbeaten closed at full speed.

1150 B/16 - The faint outline of a merchant ship was visible on bearing 290° at a range of about 5 miles. Unbeaten continued to close at full speed but an attack in such poor visibility was impossible from long range. Unbeaten was already abaft the targets beam. Three aircraft was patrolling nearby, obviously air escorts of this merchant vessel.
1600 B/16 - In position 38°00'N, 16°11'E sighted a CANT flying boat to the southward.
1637 B/16 - Sighted a merchant vessel escorted by a destroyer bearing 241°. Range was about 7 nautical miles. They were proceeding close inshore. Started attack.
1645 B/16 - The merchant vessel was a large one of about 11000 tons. She was now seen to be escorted by three destroyers.
1706 B/16 - Fired four torpedoes from 4000 yards.
1710 B/16 - Almost four minutes after firing 2 loud explosions were heard. HE from the target, which had been strong, stopped. Two of the destroyers made a counter attack.
1800 B/16 - Returned to periscope depth and sighted a Generali-class torpedo boat only 500 yards off. Went deep again.
1825 B/16 - Last depth charges dropped.
1835 B/16 - Two destroyers were seen stopped in the place of the attack. There was no sign of the target.
1850 B/16 - Cleared the area to the south-east. (33)
17 Mar 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian submarine Guglielmotti off Cape Dell'Armi, Italy in position 37°42'N, 15°58'E.

According to Italian sources she had been on passage from Taranto to Messina. The torpedo boat Francesco Stocco arrived on the scene, dropped 17 depth charges and picked up one body but there were no survivors.
0633 B/17 - In position 37°42'N, 15°58'E heard HE bearing 130°.
0635 B/17 - Sighted a submarine bearing 125°, distant 2200 yards, manoeuvred into attack position.
0640 B/17 - Fired four torpedoes. One minute and 40 seconds after firing an explosion was heard, HE stopped and the submarine was heard breaking up.
0720 B/17 - Surfaced to pick up survivors. There were about 12 in the water but Unbeaten was forced to dive by an approaching aircraft and clear the area.
1005 B/17 - Aircraft and motor torpedo boats were seen in the area of the sinking.
1010 B/17 to 1020 B/17 - Distant depth charging was heard. Twenty-four depth charges were dropped by the three motor torpedo boats present.
Unbeaten was now out of torpedoes so course was set to Malta to take on board new torpedoes. (33)

19 Mar 1942 Around 0830 B/19, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) arrived at Malta to take on board new torpedoes. Around 2000 B/19 she departed again to resume her patrol. She was to patrol in position 37°47'N, 15°45'E. En-route to that position she was to pass through position 37°10'N, 16°02'E. (33)
21 Mar 1942 At 0632 B/21, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) was in position 37°37'N, 15°56'E when HE was heard bearing 200° which was closed. It was still very dark.
At 0640 B/21, smoke was sighted bearing 200°. It was closed at speed.
At 0650 B/21, a 3500 tons transport and two destroyers were sighted bearing 195°. Enemy course was about 100°. Range was 17000 yards. The attack was broken off as the enemy was well out of range.

This was most probably the German transport Brook (1225 GRT, built 1927) escorted by the Italian destroyer Strale and the Italian torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari which sailed from Messina at 0015 B/21-0100 B/21. They were attacked by torpedo bombers at 0415 B/21, 18 miles south of Cape dell’Armi. They arrived at Argostoli at 0845 B/22.
At 1600 B/21, a Generali-class torpedo boat was sighted proceeding westwards close inshore along the Calabrian coast.
[We have been unable to identify this vessels, no destroyers or torpedo boats should have been in the area at this time.]

At 2240 B/21, HE of a single destroyer was picked up which appeared to be patrolling in the vicinity of position 37°52'N, 15°57'E some 12 miles, 110° from Cape dell'Armi. This destroyer remained on patrol until 0100 B/22. Unbeaten retired to the southward to charge.
This was most probably HE from the Italian torpedo boat Francesco Stocco which sailed from Messina at 2045 B/21 for an A/S sweep and returned at 1045 B/22. (33)

24 Mar 1942 At 1706 B/24, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1426 B/24, which ordered her to leave patrol leave patrol so as to arrive off Malta A.M. on the 26th. She was to proceed through position 37°15'N, 16°02'E on the way back to Malta.
HMS Unbeaten immediately altered course for Malta to comply with these orders. (39)

25 Mar 1942 At 0140 B/25, (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0040 B/25, 0109 B/25 and 0121 B/25 which stated the following; 0040 B/25; Two enemy cruisers at 2350 B/24 in position 36°25'N, 16°48'E, course 330° speed 15 knots. (this position was amended later in the signal timed 0121 B/25, see below). Albacores will attack around 0300 B/25.
0109 B/25; My 0040 B/25, endeavour to intercept.
0121 B/25; My 0040 B/25, amend position to 36°45'N, 16°45'E.
HMS Unbeaten immediately altered course to 020° and increased speed to full. Weather was bad so speed made good was only 7 knots.
At 0310 B/25, in position 37°03'N, 15°54'E, sighted aircraft flares bearing 110° well over the horizon. Course was altered to 055° to try to intercept.
The bearing of the flares, which were dropped intermittently maintained a steady bearing of 110° but remained well over the horizon.
At 0415 B/25, the last flare was sighted.
At 0545 B/25, the chase was abandoned and dived as dawn was approaching. Nothing was in sight and no HE was picked up after diving.

No enemy cruisers had been in this area. These were the Italian destroyer Folgore and the Italian torpedo boat Pallade searching for survivors of the destroyers Lanciere and Scirocco which had foundered in bad weather.
At 2340 B/25, HMS Unbeaten passed her signal timed 2142 B/25 to Capt. S.10. This signal contained her time of arrival at Malta which was given as 0900 B/27. A weather report was also included. (39)
26 Mar 1942 Around 0900 B/26, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) ended her 19th war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (33)

1 Apr 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) was damaged by enemy bombing while laying submerged at Malta. The damage could not be repaired at Malta due to the heavy air raids and it was decided that Unbeaten had to go to Gibraltar for docking and repairs.

Damage to HMS Unbeaten consisted of one cracked container, number thre bow cap leaking badly, number one slightly. Dent in pressure hull plate and leaking rivet in tube space and stowage compartment. Small leak in number one battery sump which necessitates docking for about 2 days.
It was intended to sail her to Gibraltar, from where after a docking and rest period, the Capt. S.8 was to sail her on patrol in the Gulf of Genoa and thence via west Coast Sardinia to Malta. (5)

9 Apr 1942 At 0530 B/9, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) departed Malta for Gibraltar. En-route she was to make rendezvous with HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) to take on board Capt. Wilson, RM and transport him to Gibraltar.
[No log is available for this period so no map of the passage to the U.K. can be displayed.] (5)

11 Apr 1942 At 0340 B/11, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) made rendezvous with HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) in position 35°34'N, 12°17'E. She then took on board Capt. Wilson, RM and proceeded towards Gibraltar. The crew of HMS Unbeaten were the last to see HMS Upholder which did not return from her patrol. (5)

12 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1508 B/12, F.O.C.N.A. (Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic), informed HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) of her onward route to Gibraltar; From 38°20'N, 09°00'E at 1200 B/14 to 38°12'N, 01°01'E at 0600 B/18 then to 36°31'N, 02°12'W at 0330 B/20 to arrive Gibraltar Gate at 1200 B/21. Do not pass 05°00'W before 0830 B/21. Report if unable to comply within 3 hours. Bombing restrictions are arranged.
At 1745 B/12, in position 37°24'N, 12°29'E, the mast of a northbound merchant vessel were seen. Two fighters appeared to be overhead as air escorts.

This was most probably the Italian transport Gino Allegri (6836 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Centauro which sailed from Tripoli at 1900 B/11. At 1412 B/12, they were near Pantelleria where they temporary picked up an additional escort of the motor torpedo boats MAS 557 and MAS 564 which had sailed from Pantelleria at 1330 B/12 and returned at 1640 B/12. Ginp Allegri and Centauro arrived at Naples at 1057 B/13. (40)

16 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1308 B/16, F.O.C.N.A. (Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic) sent the following orders to HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN): From 38°12'N, 01°01'E amend route to pass through 36°35'N, 02°12'W at 0330 B/20 and then through 36°27'N, 04°27'E at 0430 B/21 thence on the surface to arrive off Gibraltar gate at 1200 B/21. (41)

19 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 2111 B/19, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) sent the following signal to F.O.C.N.A. (Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic); E.T.A. 36°27'N, 04°27'N at 0900 B/21. Propose diving at 0001 B/22 in 36°14'N, 05°00' surfacing 180° - Europa Point - 1 mile at 0930 B/22. Request approval own arrangements.
F.O.C.N.A. replied in a signal timed 2340 B/19 as follows; Your 2111 B/19 not approved for operational reasons. Proceed on surface from 36 27'N, 04°27'W direct to arrive Gibraltar 1600 B/21. (41)

21 Apr 1942 Around 1830 B/21, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) arrived at Gibraltar for repairs before being able to be deployed operationally. She was taken in hand for repairs on April 29th.

On docking it however became apparent that it was not considered wise to sent HMS Unbeaten on patrol until permanent damage repairs had been made. It was decided that she was to proceed to the U.K. to be taken in hand for refit at the Chatham Dockyard which now had capacity available as HMS Upholder which was supposed to have refitted there had not returned from patrol earlier in April. (5)
26 May 1942 Around 1500 B/26, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) departed Gibraltar for the U.K.
She was to cross 43°N in 12°10'W at 0800 B/1.
From there she was to proceed dived by day through positions 46°01'N, 12°10'W, 49°05'N, 12°06'N. 49°30'N, 08°01'W to R/V as ordered by the Capt. S.5 in the vicinity of Lizard Head on 8 June.
In a signal timed 1551 B/2, the Capt. S.5 ordered her to make rendezvous in position 180° - Lizard Head - 5 miles at 0700 B/8.
In a signal timed 1221 B/4, the Capt. S.5 amended the rendezvous position to 180° - Lizard Head - 10 miles.
In a signal timed 0015 B/6, HMS Unbeaten informed the Capt. S.5 that she was not able to make the rendezvous at the time ordered and she requested instructions.

In a signal timed 1221 B/4, the Capt. S.5 sent her the following orders; Amend route to pass through positions 180° - Bishop Rock - 15 miles and 180° - Lizard Head - 15 miles, thence to the rendezvous at 1030 B/7. (10)

7 Jun 1942 Around 1000 B/7 - 1130 B/7, HMS Torbay (Cdr. A.C.C. Miers, DSO and Bar, RN) and HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) made rendezvous in position 180° - Lizard Head - 10 miles, with their escort towards Portsmouth HMS Kingston Jacinth (Skr. A.G. Day, RNR). (42)
8 Jun 1942 Around 0930 B/8, HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) arrived at Portsmouth (Gosport / Fort Blockhouse) from Gibraltar. (10)

12 Jun 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) departed Portsmouth for Sheerness. She took passage in convoy CE 89 (St. Helens Roads - Southend) which was escorted by HMS Garth (Lt.Cdr. J.P. Scatchard, RN). (5)
13 Jun 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) arrived at Sheerness. She proceeded to Chatham Dockyard later the same day escorted by HM ML 113 (?). (43)
14 Jun 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) started her refit at Chatham Dockyard. (5)
15 Sep 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) ended her refit at the Chatham Dockyard.
[As no logs are available for HMS Unbeaten from February 1942 until her loss, details will be missing.] (5)

17 Sep 1942 With her refit at the Chatham dockyard completed HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) departed Sheerness around 1345 A/17 for Portsmouth. She made the passage together with HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) which had just completed damage repairs at the Chatham Dockyard. They were escorted by the barrage balloon vessel HMS Bungay (T/Lt. G.D. Davies, RNR). (44)
18 Sep 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) and HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) arrive at Portsmouth (Gosport, HMS Dolphin) from Sheerness. (45)
21 Sep 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) departed Portsmouth for Plymouth. She was escorted by HMS Lord Stonehaven (Skr. A.F.S. Jenner, RNR). (45)
22 Sep 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) arrived at Plymouth. At Plymouth (Devonport Dockyard) a radar set was fitted and R.D.F. trials followed on completion. (45)

4 Oct 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) departed Plymouth for Holy Loch. She was escorted to the vicinity of Trevose Head by HMS Cambridgeshire (T/Lt. H.F.B. Ashby, RNVR) where HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR) took over. (5)
6 Oct 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) arrived at Holy Loch to begin a period of training.
[As no log is available no details can be given.] (45)

23 Oct 1942 Around 1700 A/23, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) departed Holy Loch for her 20th war patrol. This was the 1st patrol of her 2nd commission. She was ordered to patrol in the Bay of Biscay (off Northern Spain) and to perform a special operation (Operation Bluestone), landing a special agent near Baiona, Spain (near Vigo).
Passage south through the Irish Sea was made together with HMS P 614 (Lt. H.W. Wilkinson, RN). The submarines were escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN).
The submarines parted company with the escort around 2110 A/25 when west of Bishop Rock.
From there HMS Unbeaten was to proceed to her patrol area through positions; (E) 50°01'N, 08°01'W. (F) 50°01'N, 13°01'W. (G) 45°01'N, 13°01'W. (H) 43°30'N, 12°01'W, E.T.A. 0600 A/30. (46)

2 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1930 A/2, to the Flag Officer Submarines HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) informed him that the special operation had been carried out at 2359 A/1. Un the signal it was also stated that at 1000 A/2 a ship similar in appearance to the Portuguese Angola (7745 GRT, built 1912) wearing Spanish markings had been seen, west of Vigo, in position 42°19'N, 10°00'W proceeding on course 020° at 10 knots. HMS Unbeaten had been unable to identify this ship positively.
In a signal timed 2212 A/2, F.O.S. replied as follows; Your 1930 A/2. Proceed forthwith diving by day to area K 501 for operations in accordance with your patrol orders. (47)

6 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1141 A/6, the Flag Officer Submarines sent the following orders to HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN); Reported at 1100 A/6 a 6000-ton merchant ship in position 44°58'N, 05°10'W proceeding on course 270°, speed 12 knots. Proceed to intercept, bombing restrictions extended.
In a signal timed 1334 A/6, F.O.S. informed HMS Unbeaten that aircraft report the enemy merchant ship at 1221 A/6 now in position 44°46'N, 05°43'W, course 270°, speed 10 knots.

In a signal timed 1522 A/6, F.O.S. informed HMS Unbeaten that the merchant vessel's position at 1340 A/6 was 44°56'N, 05°58'W and that the enemy's speed may be reduced. Three aircraft strikes have been arranged in Bay of Biscay area.

At 1920 A/6, a signal timed 1810 A/6, was received which reported that an enemy supply vessel had been sighted in position 45°00'N, 07°00'W on bearing 055° and at a range of 7 miles. Enemy course was 135°, speed 11 knots. The signal was a bit garbled but it is presumed this signal came from HMS Unbeaten as no other HM ship was to be in that position at that time.
In a signal timed 2109 A/6, F.O.S. sent the following orders to HMS Unbeaten; If no contact has been made with enemy by dawn 7th November proceed to patrol vicinity of position 45°01'N, 07°59'W.
These signals referred to the German blockade breaker Anneliese Essberger (5173 GRT, built 1935) which had left the Gironde estuary in the evening of 5 November 1942 for the Far East. (48)

7 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1901 A/7, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) received the following signal from the Capt. S.3; leave patrol area after dark 8th, dive by day and pass through the following positions at times stated; (A) 44°31'N, 06°59'W at 0030 A/9. (B) 45°05'N, 04°02'W at 0030 A/10. (C) 47°55'N, 07°59'W at 2000 A/11. (D) 180° - Bishops Rock - 10 miles at 1700 A/12. In position D rendezvous was to be made with HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN). (10)

11 Nov 1942 At 0216 A/11, a submarine was detected steering 330° to 340° in position 46°55'N, 06°51'W by Wellington 'F' of No.172 squadron RAF Coastal Command. This S/M was attacked at 0235 A/11. She was straddled with depth charges and attacked with machine guns. 30 minutes later the attacking aircraft reported that the submarine had turned 90° to starboard and was stationary.

The attack position is 12 miles inside a submerged bombing restrictions area with total bombing restrictions at night. The attack position was only 10 miles from the estimated position by the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) of HMS Unbeaten.
The attacking aircraft also noticed radar interference. HMS Unbeaten was equipped with radar.

No German submarine was missing following this attack and it is very likely the submarine attacked and sunk was HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) which should have been in this area around this time.
The aircraft was not aware of the bombing restrictions which were in place.
In a signal timed 0514 A/11 the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) informed HMS Unbeaten of this attack.

In a signal timed 0739 A/11, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) informed HMS Unbeaten that a Sunderland aircraft escorted by two Beaufighters were proceeding towards position 46°55'N, 06°51'W with object of escorting you should you be unable to dive.
From the above signal it is apparent that it must have been realised that the submarine attacked by the aircraft may have been HMS Unbeaten.
In a signal timed 1319 A/11, F.O.S. ordered HMS Unbeaten to report her position, course and speed. The signal was transmitted four timed but there came no reply.
Aircraft searches on 11, 12, 13 and 14 November failed to find any sign of the submarine or it's crew. (48)
ADM numbers indicate documents at the British National Archives at Kew, London.
